title: EXPOSÉ VIII COMPLEMENTS ON BIEXTENSIONS. GENERAL PROPERTIES OF BIEXTENSIONS OF GROUP SCHEMES

pages.title: EXPOSÉ VIII COMPLEMENTS ON BIEXTENSIONS. GENERAL PROPERTIES OF BIEXTENSIONS OF GROUP SCHEMES

blocks.html:
EXPOSÉ VIII
COMPLEMENTS ON BIEXTENSIONS. GENERAL PROPERTIES
OF BIEXTENSIONS OF GROUP SCHEMESby A. Grothendieck
CONTENTS
0. Introduction
1. Various special cases over an arbitrary topos
2. Pairings defined by a biextension
3. Biextensions of group schemes \((P,Q)\) by \(G_m\): generalities
4. Extensions and biextensions of smooth connected group schemes over a field
5. Extensions and biextensions by truncated constant group schemes without torsion
6. Extensions and biextensions by the Néron model of \(G_m\)
7. Canonical prolongations of extensions and of biextensions by \(G_m\)
0. Introduction

0.1. We continue here the general study of biextensions, begun in the preceding exposé. First of all we give some complements in the case of a general base topos, developing in particular, with the details it deserves, the formalism of the pairings associated with biextensions (n°2), whose importance in the theory of abelian schemes is well known. On the other hand, we take up the study of biextensions of general group schemes, the most interesting case for us being that of biextensions by the multiplicative group \(G_m\). We shall however be obliged, as a technical intermediary, to study also biextensions by other types of group schemes (n°s 5 and 6).

The most interesting result for the sequel is given in 7.1, which gives conditions under which, for \(P\) and \(Q\) smooth over a trait with generic point \(\eta\), a biextension of \((P_\eta,Q_\eta)\) by \(G_{m\eta}\) can be prolonged to a biextension of \((P,Q)\) by \(G_m\), determined up to unique isomorphism. This result will be very convenient in the following exposé, devoted to the theory of the Néron model of abelian varieties. It is probable that one could get by there at a better price, without the imposing paperweight of exposés VII and VIII (which will no longer be used in the rest of the Seminar); it seems probable to us however that the developments given in these exposés are destined to be useful elsewhere as well.

0.2. When we work with group schemes over a scheme \(S\), it will be understood that we work with the fppf topos of \(S\), which is the topos associated with the \(U\)-site of schemes locally of finite presentation over \(S\) (and \(\in U\)), equipped with the fppf topology (SGA 3 IV 6.3). The reader will moreover verify without difficulty that most of the statements we shall give would be equally valid (and essentially equivalent to the fppf statements) for other topologies on \(S\) (having as category underlying the site a category containing at least the schemes locally of finite presentation over \(S\)), such as the Zariski topology, the étale topology, the fpqc topology. One of the reasons for this is that, thanks to descent theory, the category of extensions of a group scheme \(P\) locally of finite presentation over \(S\) by the group scheme \(G_m\) does not depend, up to equivalence, on the topology chosen, and the same statement holds for the notion of biextension. The technical reason why the fppf topology (or a finer topology, such as fpqc) is to be preferred to the coarser topologies such as the Zariski one and the étale topology, is that for every integer \(n>0\), the homomorphism \(n.\mathrm{id}\) of \(G_m\) into itself is an fppf epimorphism (for it is faithfully flat of finite presentation), but in general not an epimorphism (Zar) or (ét). (NB it is an epimorphism (ét) if and only if \(n\) is prime to the residue characteristics of \(S\) and an epimorphism (Zar) if and only if \(S\) is empty.)

0.3. Let us point out finally that most of the statements concerning extensions or biextensions by \(G_m\) would remain valid on replacing this group by a torus \(G\). As such a \(G\) is locally isomorphic (for fppf, and even for (ét)) to a group \(G_m^r\), this is an essentially trivial generalization.

pages.title: 1. Various special cases over an arbitrary topos

blocks.html: 1. Various special cases over an arbitrary topos
1.1. In the present section and the following one, as in VII, \(T\) denotes a topos, \(P,Q\) and \(G\) denote three abelian Groups of \(T\), so that we have a canonical isomorphism (VII 3.6.5):
\[(1.1.1)\qquad \operatorname{Biext}^1(P,Q;G)\simeq \operatorname{Ext}^1(P\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathrm L}}Q,G).\]
Recall that we have canonical isomorphisms (called "Cartan" isomorphisms)
\[(1.1.2)\qquad \operatorname{Ext}^1(P\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathrm L}}Q,G)\simeq \operatorname{Ext}^1(P,\mathrm{RHom}(Q,G))\simeq \operatorname{Ext}^1(Q,\mathrm{RHom}(P,G)).\]
All these isomorphisms are functorial in the three arguments \(P,Q,G\).

From (1.1.1) one deduces the following infinite exact sequence, taking into account that the homology objects of \(P\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathrm L}}Q\) are the \(\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_i(P,Q)\), which are null for \(i\geq2\):

\[(1.1.3)\qquad 0\longrightarrow \operatorname{Ext}^1(P\otimes Q,G)\longrightarrow \operatorname{Biext}^1(P,Q;G)\longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}(\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1(P,Q),G)\longrightarrow\] \[\operatorname{Ext}^2(P\otimes Q,G)\longrightarrow \operatorname{Ext}^2(P\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathrm L}}Q,G)\longrightarrow\cdots .\]
On the other hand, the cohomology objects of \(\mathrm{RHom}(Q,G)\) being the \(\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^i(Q,G)\), the first equality (1.1.2) gives rise to the 5-term exact sequence,

\[(1.1.4)\qquad 0\longrightarrow \operatorname{Ext}^1(P,\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(Q,G))\longrightarrow \operatorname{Biext}^1(P,Q;G)\longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}(P,\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^1(Q,G))\] \[\longrightarrow \operatorname{Ext}^2(P,\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(Q,P))\longrightarrow \operatorname{Ext}^2(P,\mathrm{RHom}(Q,G)),\]
associated with the usual spectral sequence
\[\operatorname{Ext}^*(P,\mathrm{RHom}(Q,G))\Longleftarrow \operatorname{Ext}^p(P,\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^q(Q,G)).\]
We leave to the reader the task of making precise, if he feels the need, the first arrow of (1.1.3) (resp. of (1.1.4)) by means of a natural fully faithful functor
\[(1.1.5)\qquad \operatorname{EXT}(P\otimes Q,G)\longrightarrow \operatorname{BIEXT}(P,Q;G)\]
(resp.
\[(1.1.6)\qquad \operatorname{EXT}(P,\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(Q,G))\longrightarrow \operatorname{BIEXT}(P,Q;G)),\]
where \(\operatorname{EXT}\) and \(\operatorname{BIEXT}\) denote the categories of extensions and of biextensions that one guesses.

Of course, the isomorphism \(\operatorname{Biext}^1(P,Q;G)\simeq \operatorname{Ext}^1(Q,\mathrm{RHom}(P,G))\) gives rise to an exact sequence analogous to (1.1.3), with the roles of \(P\) and \(Q\) interchanged, whose first arrow can be made precise by means of a fully faithful functor
\[(1.1.7)\qquad \operatorname{EXT}(Q,\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(P,G))\longrightarrow \operatorname{BIEXT}(P,Q;G)\]
analogous to (1.1.6).
1.2. Suppose that we have
\[(1.2.1)\qquad \underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1(P,Q)=0,\]
then (1.1.3) furnishes a canonical isomorphism
\[(1.2.2)\qquad \operatorname{Biext}^1(P,Q;G)\simeq \operatorname{Ext}^1(P\otimes Q,G).\]

This is the case for example if \(P\) or \(Q\) is a flat \(\mathbb Z\)-Module. One can evidently sharpen this statement by saying that under the condition (1.2.1), the functor (1.1.5) is an equivalence of categories.
1.3. Suppose that we have
\[(1.3.1)\qquad P\otimes Q=0,\]
then (1.1.3) furnishes a canonical isomorphism
\[(1.3.2)\qquad \operatorname{Biext}^1(P,Q;G)\simeq \operatorname{Hom}(\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1(P,Q),G).\]
The most interesting case for us where (1.3.1) is satisfied is the one where we have at our disposal an integer \(n>0\) such that we have
\[(1.3.3)\qquad nP=P,\qquad Q=\varinjlim_i\,{}_{n^i}Q,\]
i.e. \(P\) is divisible by \(n\) and \(Q\) is a limit of sub-Groups annihilated by powers of \(n\). Setting, following usage
\[T_n(P)=({}_{n^i}P)_{i\geq0}\quad\text{(projective system)},\qquad P_{n^\infty}=\varinjlim_i\,{}_{n^i}P,\]
one concludes from (1.3.3) formulas more precise than (1.3.1):
\[(1.3.4)\qquad P\otimes Q=0,\qquad \underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1(P,Q)\simeq \varinjlim_i\,{}_{n^i}P\otimes{}_{n^i}Q\simeq T_n(P)\otimes Q\simeq P_{n^\infty}\otimes T_n(Q),\]
so that the isomorphism (1.3.2) here takes the more concrete form:
\[(1.3.5)\quad \operatorname{Biext}^1(P,Q;G)\simeq \operatorname{Hom}(T_n(P),D(Q))\simeq \operatorname{Hom}(Q,D(T_n(P)))\simeq \operatorname{Hom}(T_n(P)\otimes T_n(Q),T_n(G)).\]
Let us make precise the meaning of these formulas:
a) In the inductive system which occurs in (1.3.4), the transition homomorphism
\[{}_{n^i}P\otimes{}_{n^i}Q\longrightarrow{}_{n^j}P\otimes{}_{n^j}Q\qquad(i\leq j)\]
is described by identifying the first member with \({}_{n^j}P\otimes{}_{n^i}Q\) thanks to \(n^{j-i}\otimes\mathrm{id}\), and by using the homomorphism
\[\mathrm{id}\otimes\mathrm{inj}:{}_{n^j}P\otimes{}_{n^i}Q \longrightarrow{}_{n^j}P\otimes{}_{n^j}Q,\]
where \(\mathrm{inj}:{}_{n^i}Q\longrightarrow{}_{n^j}Q\) denotes the inclusion.
b) One sets, by definition
\[T_n(P)\otimes Q\mathrel{\mathop=^{\rm dfn}} \varinjlim_i T_n(P)\otimes{}_{n^i}Q,\]
where
\[T_n(P)\otimes M\mathrel{\mathop=^{\rm dfn}}{}_{n^i}P\otimes M \quad\text{if}\quad n^iM=0.\]

One makes precise in the same way the meaning of the third term written in (1.3.4), when one assumes that \(nQ=Q\) (so that \(T_n(Q)\) is, like \(T_n(P)\), a strict projective system). The last term of (1.3.4) is a Hom of projective systems of groups.
c) The letter \(D\) denotes \(\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(-,G)\). The object \(D(Q)\) is defined as the projective system of Groups
\[D(Q)=\bigl(D({}_{n^i}Q)\bigr)_{i\geq0} =\bigl(\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}({}_{n^i}Q,G)\bigr)_{i\geq0},\]
the second member of (1.3.5) being the group of homomorphisms of projective systems of groups. The last member can be interpreted more simply by setting
\[D(T_n(P))=\varinjlim_i D({}_{n^i}P),\]
the transition morphisms of this inductive system being evidently those which are deduced by transposition from the transition morphisms
\[n^{j-i}:{}_{n^j}P\longrightarrow{}_{n^i}P\quad\text{of }T_n(P).\]

We leave to the reader the task of making explicit the computation of \(\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1(P,Q)\) indicated in (1.3.4), and the consequence (1.3.5) of this computation. Let us note only that one uses the relations deduced from the second hypothesis (1.3.3):
\[\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_p(P,Q)\simeq \varinjlim_i\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_p(P,{}_{n^i}Q),\]

which for \(p=0\) immediately gives \(P\otimes Q=0\), and which reduces the computation of the \(\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1(P,Q)\) to that of the \(\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1(P,{}_{n^i}Q)\) and of their transition morphisms. It remains therefore, when \(Q\) is annihilated by an integer \(m=n^i\) such that \(mP=P\), to make explicit an isomorphism
\[(1.3.6)\qquad \underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1(P,Q) \xleftarrow{\sim}{}_{m}P\otimes_m Q\qquad(mP=P,\quad mQ=0).\]
There are various ways of doing this, one of them consisting in using the exact sequence of the \(\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}\) associated with the exact sequence
\[0\longrightarrow{}_{m}P\longrightarrow P\xrightarrow{m}P\longrightarrow0.\]

One can also use the homomorphism (2.1.1) below; the fact that the transition morphisms are those made precise in a) is then none other than 2.1.11. Let us point out that in any case, the choice of (1.3.6) amounts to the choice of a sign, cf. 2.1.10.
1.4. Suppose that we have
\[(1.4.1)\qquad \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(Q,G)=0,\]
then (1.1.4) furnishes a canonical isomorphism
\[(1.4.2)\qquad \operatorname{Biext}^1(P,Q;G)\xrightarrow{\sim} \operatorname{Hom}(P,\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^1(Q,G)).\]

This case occurs in particular if \(Q\) is an abelian scheme and if \(G=G_m\), cf. VIII 3.8. Hence the functor in \(P\), \(P\longmapsto\operatorname{Biext}^1(P,Q;G)\) is representable by \(Q^*=\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^1(Q,G)\), and one finds in particular a canonical biextension \(E^Q\) of \((Q^*,Q)\) by \(G\), defined up to unique isomorphism (since the group of automorphisms of \(E^Q\) is \(\operatorname{Hom}(P,\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(Q,G))=0\). One may call this biextension, or the symmetric biextension \({}^sE^Q\) of \((Q,Q^*)\) by \(G\), the Weil biextension of \((Q^*,Q)\) or \((Q,Q^*)\) by \(G\).
1.5. Suppose that we have
\[(1.5.1)\qquad \operatorname{Hom}(P,\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^1(Q,G))=0,\]

which is the case in particular if \(\operatorname{Ext}^1(Q,G)=0\) (for example if \(Q\) is a finite locally free group scheme or a group of multiplicative type (SGA 3 IX), and \(G=G_m\), cf. 3.4 below). Then (1.1.4) furnishes a canonical isomorphism
\[(1.5.2)\qquad \operatorname{Biext}^1(P,Q;G)\xleftarrow{\sim} \operatorname{Ext}^1(P,\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(Q,G)),\]

where \(\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(Q,G)\) plays the role of a « dual group » of \(Q\) (relative to \(G\)). More precisely, one deduces from 1.5.2 that the fully faithful functor (1.1.6) is an equivalence of categories.
1.6. Suppose that we have simultaneously the relations
\[(1.6.1)\qquad \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(P,G)=0,\quad \operatorname{Hom}(P,\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^1(Q,G))=0.\]
By virtue of 1.4, these two relations imply respectively that we have isomorphisms

\[\operatorname{Biext}^1(P,Q;G)\xrightarrow{\sim} \operatorname{Hom}(Q,\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^1(P,G)),\] \[\operatorname{Biext}^1(P,Q;G)\xleftarrow{\sim} \operatorname{Ext}^1(P,\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(Q,G)),\]
whence, by composing, a canonical isomorphism
\[(1.6.2)\qquad \operatorname{Ext}^1(P,\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(Q,G)) \xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Hom}(Q,\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^1(P,G)) \quad(\simeq\operatorname{Biext}^1(P,G;G)),\]

playing the role of a duality formula. Let us note moreover that the first relation (1.6.1) evidently implies that \(\operatorname{Hom}(P\otimes Q,G)=0\), hence that the category \(\operatorname{EXT}(P,\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(Q,G))\) of (commutative) extensions of \(P\) by \(\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(Q,G)\) is rigid (every automorphism of an object is the identity), and that the same is true of \(\operatorname{BIEXT}(P,Q;G)\). The structure of these categories is thus known when one knows the group of isomorphism classes of objects, which by virtue of (1.6.2) is canonically isomorphic to \(\operatorname{Hom}(Q,\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^1(P,Q))\).

Let us note that the map (1.6.2) is defined in any case (without assuming (1.6.1)), and that it can also be described directly, without passing through \(\operatorname{Biext}^1(P,Q;G)\), in terms of the natural pairing
\[Q\times Q'\longrightarrow G\]
(where \(Q'=\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(Q,G)\)), by interpreting it as a homomorphism \(Q\to\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(Q',G)\), whence a composite
\[(1.6.3)\qquad \operatorname{Ext}^1(P,Q')\times Q \longrightarrow\operatorname{Ext}^1(P,Q')\times \operatorname{Hom}(Q',G)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Ext}^1(P,G),\]
which gives back (1.6.2).
For a useful case where the considerations of 1.6 apply, see 3.7 below.
2. Pairings defined by a biextension

2.1. Let \(P\) and \(Q\) be two abelian Groups over \(T\), and \(n>0\) an integer. Recall that one denotes by \({}_nP\) the kernel of multiplication by \(n\,\mathrm{id}_P\). We shall define a canonical homomorphism
\[(2.1.1)\qquad \alpha=\alpha^n_{P,Q}:{}_{n}P\otimes{}_{n}Q \longrightarrow\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1(P,Q),\]
which will be the composite
\[(2.1.2)\qquad {}_{n}P\otimes{}_{n}Q\longrightarrow \underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1({}_{n}P,{}_{n}Q) \longrightarrow\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1(P,Q),\]

where the first arrow is \(\alpha^n_{{}_nP,{}_nQ}\), and the second is the one deduced from the inclusions \({}_nP\to P\), \({}_nQ\to Q\). We are thus reduced to defining (2.1.1) in the case where we have \(nP=0\), \(nQ=0\), i.e. where \(P\) and \(Q\) are \(\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z\)-Modules. Suppose only \(nQ=0\). Taking a flat resolution \(L\) of \(P\) (as a \(\mathbb Z\)-Module), one finds canonical isomorphisms
\[P\mathbin{\mathop\otimes^L}_{\mathbb Z}Q\simeq L\otimes_{\mathbb Z}Q\simeq (L\otimes_{\mathbb Z}\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z) \otimes_{\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z}Q,\]
which can be written as an isomorphism in the derived category
\[(2.1.3)\qquad P\mathbin{\mathop\otimes^L}_{\mathbb Z}Q\simeq (P\mathbin{\mathop\otimes^L}_{\mathbb Z}\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z) \mathbin{\mathop\otimes^L}_{\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z}Q,\]
giving rise to the « Kunneth » spectral sequence of homological type (cf. SGA 1 IV 5.2):

\[(2.1.4)\qquad \underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_*^{\mathbb Z}(P,Q) \Longleftarrow E^2_{p,q}= \underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_p^{\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z} (\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_q^{\mathbb Z}(P,\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z),Q).\]
Recall moreover that we have canonical isomorphisms

\[\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_0^{\mathbb Z}(P,\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z) \mathrel{\mathop=^{\rm dfn}}P_n=P/nP,\qquad \underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1(P,\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z)\simeq{}_nP,\] \[\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_i^{\mathbb Z}(P,\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z)=0 \quad\text{if }i\geq2,\]
deduced from the computation of the \(\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_i^{\mathbb Z} (P,\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z)\) by means of the following canonical flat resolution of \((\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z)_T\):
\[\xymatrix{0\ar[r]&\mathbb Z_T\ar[r]^n\ar@{=}[d]& \mathbb Z_T\ar[r]\ar@{=}[d]&0\\&L_1&L_0&},\]
the augmentation \(L_0\longrightarrow(\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z)_T\) being defined by sending 1 to 1. Conse- quently, the spectral sequence (2.1.4) reduces essentially to an exact sequence

\[(2.1.5)\quad 0\longrightarrow \underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_2^{\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z}(P_n,Q) \longrightarrow P_n\otimes Q\mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{\alpha}} \underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1^{\mathbb Z}(P,Q) \longrightarrow\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1^{\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z}(P_n,Q) \longrightarrow0,\]

where we have set \(P_n=P\otimes_{\mathbb Z}\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z=P/nP\). The homomorphism \(\alpha\) sought is the edge homomorphism \(E^2_{0,1}\longrightarrow\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1^{\mathbb Z}\) which figures in the last exact sequence. The latter being evidently functorial in \(P\) and in \(Q\), the functoriality in \(P\) shows that the homomorphism under consideration is indeed the composite
\[{}_nP\otimes Q\longrightarrow \underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1^{\mathbb Z}({}_nP,Q) \longrightarrow\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1^{\mathbb Z}(P,Q),\]

where the first homomorphism is the homomorphism \(\alpha\) defined as before, but with \((P,Q)\) replaced by \(({}_nP,Q)\), and where the second homomorphism comes from the inclusion \({}_nP\longrightarrow P\).

2.1.6. This therefore indeed defines, for \(P\) and \(Q\) arbitrary, a homomorphism (2.1.1) satisfying the factorization condition (2.1.2). Moreover, for \(nQ=0\), the exact sequence (2.1.5) shows that \(\alpha\) is an isomorphism as soon as \(P_n\) is flat over \(\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z\) (which is the case in particular if \(P_n=0\), i.e. \(nP=P\) i.e. \(P\) is \(n\)-divisible, or if \(P\) itself is a flat \(\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z\)-Module) or if \(Q\) is a flat \(\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z\)-Module.

2.1.7. Let us make explicit the computation of the homomorphism \(\alpha\) of (2.1.5). To know it, it suffices to know, for every section \(q\) of \(Q\) (over an object \(S\) of \(T\), say the final object to simplify) the corresponding homomorphism \({}_nP\longrightarrow\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1^{\mathbb Z}(P,Q)\). Now one can consider \(q\) as the image of the section 1 of \((\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z)_T\) by a well-determined homomorphism \(u_q:(\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z)_T\longrightarrow Q\), and the functoriality of the homomorphism \(\alpha\) in \(Q\) thus reduces us to the case where \(Q\) is the constant sheaf \((\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z)_T\), and where \(q\) is the unit section of \((\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z)_T\). In this case, identifying
\[\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1^{\mathbb Z}(P,Q)= \underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1^{\mathbb Z}(P,\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z)\quad\text{with }{}_nP\]

as explained after (2.1.4) above, the homomorphism sought is the identity, in other words, the edge homomorphism \[E^2_{0,q}=\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_q^{\mathbb Z}(P,\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z) \otimes_{\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z}\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z \longrightarrow\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_q^{\mathbb Z}(P,\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z)\] is the identity. This follows in fact immediately from the definitions (and the analogous assertion remains valid on replacing \(\mathbb Z\) and its quotient \(\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z\) by any Ring \(A\) over \(T\) and any quotient Ring \(A/I\) of \(A\)).

2.1.8. One can also determine the homomorphism \(\alpha\) of (2.1.1) by describing, for every pair of sections \(p\) of \({}_nP\) and \(q\) of \({}_nQ\) (over an object \(S\) of \(T\), which we shall take equal to the final object to simplify the notations), the section \((p,q)\) of \(\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1^{\mathbb Z}(P,Q)\). Using as above the functoriality of \(\alpha\) in \(P\) and in \(Q\), we are thus reduced to the case where we have
\[P=Q=(\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z)_T,\]
\(p\) and \(q\) being the unit section of \((\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z)_T\), and we are thus reduced to determining the homomorphism
\[\alpha:(\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z)_T\otimes(\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z)_T \longrightarrow\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1^{\mathbb Z}((\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z)_T,(\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z)_T).\]
The description 2.1.7 shows us that it is the isomorphism
\[(2.1.8.1)\qquad (\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z)_T\xrightarrow{\sim} \underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1^{\mathbb Z}((\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z)_T,(\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z)_T)\]
deduced from the canonical flat resolution
\[(2.1.8.2)\qquad 0\longrightarrow\mathbb Z_T\mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{n}}\mathbb Z_T\longrightarrow0\]
of the first factor, \(P=(\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z)_T\), which figures in the \(\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1\).
2.1.9. The definition given of (2.1.1) is not symmetric, and one can, by interchanging the roles of \(P\) and of \(Q\) in the preceding construction, define an analogous homomorphism
\[(2.1.9.1)\qquad \beta^n_{P,Q}:{}_nP\otimes{}_nQ\longrightarrow \underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1(P,Q),\]
by first reducing to the case \(nP=0\), and using a flat resolution of \(Q\). One can also define \(\beta\) in terms of \(\alpha\), in an equivalent way, by the commutativity of the diagram

\[(2.1.9.2)\qquad \xymatrix{ {{}_nP\otimes{}_nQ}\ar[r]^{\sim}\ar[d]_{\beta^n_{P,Q}}&{{}_nQ\otimes{}_nP}\ar[d]^{\alpha^n_{Q,P}}\\ {\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1(P,Q)}\ar[r]^{\sim}&{\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1(Q,P)}},\]
where the horizontal arrows are the symmetry isomorphisms. This being so, the relations between \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\) are given by the formula:
in other words:

blocks.title: Lemma 2.1.10.
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.1.10. Let \(P\) and \(Q\) be two commutative Groups, \(n>0\) an integer. Then the following diagram is anticommutative, where \(\alpha\) is defined in (2.1.1):
\[(2.1.10.1)\qquad \xymatrix{ {{}_nP\otimes{}_nQ}\ar[r]^{\sim}\ar[d]_{\alpha^n_{P,Q}}&{{}_nQ\otimes{}_nP}\ar[d]^{\alpha^n_{Q,P}}\\ {\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1(P,Q)}\ar[r]^{\sim}&{\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1(Q,P)}},\]

the horizontal arrows being the symmetry isomorphisms.

Proof. The procedure of 2.1.8 reduces us at once to the case where we have \(P=Q=(\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z)_T\), and to proving then that the two isomorphisms (2.1.8.1) obtained by resolving, via (2.1.8.2), respectively the first and the second argument occurring in the \(\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1\), yield isomorphisms which differ by the sign (or again, that the symmetry isomorphism on \(\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1^{\mathbb Z}(\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z,\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z)\) is \(-\mathrm{id}\)). One may evidently assume for this that \(T\) is the punctual topos, i.e. that one works in the category of ordinary commutative groups. Now let \(L_\bullet\) be the complex
\[L_\bullet=\quad0\longrightarrow\mathbb Z\mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{n}}\mathbb Z\longrightarrow0,\]

considered as a flat resolution of \(P=Q=\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z\). Let us use \(L_\bullet\otimes L_\bullet\) for the computation of \(\operatorname{Tor}_1(P,Q)=\operatorname{Tor}_1(\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z,\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z)\); then the isomorphisms with the \(H_1\) of \(L_\bullet\otimes\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z\) (which makes it possible to define \(\alpha\)) and with the \(H_1\) of \(\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z\otimes L_\bullet\) (which makes it possible to define \(\beta\)) are defined respectively by the augmentation \(L_\bullet\longrightarrow\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z\) of the second and of the first factor of \(L_\bullet\otimes L_\bullet\). It follows at once that \(\alpha(1,1)\) is represented by the cycle
\[-1_0\otimes1_1+1_1\otimes1_0\in(L_\bullet\otimes L_\bullet)_1 =L_0\otimes L_1+L_1\otimes L_0\]

(NB one denotes by \(1_0\) and \(1_1\) respectively the element 1 of \(L_0\) and of \(L_1\)), while \(\beta(1,1)\) is represented by the cycle \(1_0\otimes1_1-1_1\otimes1_0\). This therefore proves 2.1.10.

To finish these generalities on the pairings \(\alpha\), it remains to make precise the relations between the various homomorphisms \(\alpha^n_{P,Q}\), for variable \(n\). The principal result is given in the

blocks.title: Lemma 2.1.11.
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.1.11. Let \(P,Q\) be two commutative Groups of \(T\), \(n\) and \(n'\) two integers \(>0\) such that \(n'\) is a multiple of \(n\), say \(n'=mn\). Then we have commutativity in the following diagram
\[(2.1.11.1)\qquad \xymatrix@C=4em@R=3em{ &{{}_{n'}P\otimes{}_nQ}\ar[dl]_{p_m\otimes\mathrm{id}_{{}_nQ}}\ar[dr]^{\mathrm{id}_{{}_{n'}P}\otimes i_m}&\\ {{}_nP\otimes{}_nQ}\ar[dr]_{\alpha^n_{P,Q}}&&{{}_{n'}P\otimes{}_{n'}Q}\ar[dl]^{\alpha^{n'}_{P,Q}}\\ &{\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1(P,Q)}& }\]

where \(p_m:{}_{n'}P\longrightarrow{}_nP\) denotes the homomorphism induced by \(m.\mathrm{id}_P\), and \(i_m:{}_nQ\longrightarrow{}_{n'}Q\) denotes the inclusion. We have likewise commutativity in the analogous diagram (2.1.11.2), symmetric to (2.1.11.1), whose vertices are the same as those of (2.1.11.1), except the upper vertex \({}_{n'}P\otimes{}_nQ\), which is replaced by \({}_nP\otimes{}_{n'}Q\).

It suffices in fact to prove the first statement, the second being deduced from it by symmetry, taking 2.1.10 into account. We are reduced, by the procedure of 2.1.8, to the case where \(T\) is the punctual topos, i.e. where \(P\) and \(Q\) are ordinary commutative groups, and where we have \(P=\mathbb Z/n'\mathbb Z\), \(Q=\mathbb Z/n'\mathbb Z\). In fact, we shall use for \(P\) only the hypothesis \(n'P=0\). We shall identify \(\operatorname{Tor}_1(P,Q)\) with \({}_{n'}P=P\), thanks to the resolution \(0\longrightarrow\mathbb Z\mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{n'}}\mathbb Z\longrightarrow0\) of \(Q\), and likewise \(\operatorname{Tor}_1({}_nP,{}_nQ)\) with \({}_nP\) thanks to the resolution \(0\longrightarrow\mathbb Z\mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{n}}\mathbb Z\longrightarrow0\) of \({}_nQ\simeq\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z\), whose augmentation is given by sending \(1_0\) to \(m.1_Q\) (\(1_Q\) denoting the element 1 of \(Q=\mathbb Z/n'\mathbb Z\)). The homomorphism
\[\xymatrix{ {\operatorname{Tor}_1({}_nP,{}_nQ)}\ar[r]\ar@{<->}[d]&{\operatorname{Tor}_1(P,Q)}\ar@{<->}[d]\\ {{}_nP}&{{}_{n'}P=P} }\]

deduced from the inclusions \({}_nP\longrightarrow P\) and \({}_nQ\longrightarrow Q\) is then none other than the natural inclusion \({}_nP\longrightarrow{}_{n'}P\). Consequently, identifying the upper vertex of (2.1.11.1) with \(P/nP\), thanks to the generator \(m.1_Q\) of \({}_nQ\), which makes it possible to identify \({}_nQ\) with \(\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z\), the diagram under consideration becomes
\[\xymatrix@C=4em@R=3em{ &P/nP\ar[dl]_{x\mapsto mx}\ar[dr]^{x\mapsto mx}&\\ {{}_nP}\ar[dr]_{x\mapsto x}&&P\ar[dl]^{x\mapsto x}\\ &{{}_{n'}P}& }\]
which is evidently commutative.

blocks.title: Corollary 2.1.12.
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.1.12. With the notations of 2.1.11, we have commutativity in the following diagram
\[(2.1.12.1)\qquad \xymatrix@C=5em@R=3em{ {{}_{n'}P\otimes{}_{n'}Q}\ar[r]^{m\otimes m}\ar[d]_{\alpha^{n'}_{P,Q}}&{{}_nP\otimes{}_nQ}\ar[d]^{\alpha^n_{P,Q}}\\ {{}_{n'}\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1(P,Q)}\ar[r]^{m}&{{}_n\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1(P,Q)}},\]

where, in the description of the horizontal arrows, \(m\) denotes the homomorphisms \({}_{n'}E\longrightarrow{}_nE\) induced by \(m.\mathrm{id}_E\) (for \(E\) equal respectively to \(P,Q\) and to \(\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1(P,Q)\)).

Indeed, by virtue of 2.1.11 we shall have, for \(x\in{}_{n'}P(S)\), \(y\in{}_{n'}Q(S)\): \[\alpha^n(mx\otimes my)=\alpha^{n'}(x\otimes my),\] now the second member is equal to \(m\alpha^{n'}(x\otimes y)\) since \(x\otimes my=m(x\otimes y)\), whence the commutativity of (2.1.12.1).
2.1.13. Let \(\ell\) be a prime number. For every commutative Group \(E\), let us denote by \(T_\ell(E)\) the projective system

which evidently depends functorially on \(E\). Then the compatibilities 2.1.12, for \(n,n'\) of the form \(\ell^ u,\ell^{ u'}\) with \( u'\geq u\), show us that the \(\alpha^{\ell^ u}_{P,Q}\) for variable \( u\) define a homomorphism of projective systems, functorial in \(P,Q\):
\[(2.1.13.2)\qquad \alpha^{(\ell)}_{P,Q}:T_\ell(P)\otimes T_\ell(Q)\longrightarrow T_\ell(\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1(P,Q)).\]

The knowledge of these homomorphisms, for every prime number \(\ell\), is evidently equivalent to the knowledge of the \(\alpha^n_{P,Q}\) for every integer \(n\). The antisymmetry relations 2.1.10 are then translated into the analogous \(\ell\)-adic antisymmetry relations, for every \(\ell\), expressed by the anticommutativity of the diagrams

\[(2.1.13.3)\qquad \xymatrix@C=5em@R=3em{ T_\ell(P)\otimes T_\ell(Q)\ar[r]\ar[d]_{\alpha^{(\ell)}_{P,Q}}&T_\ell(Q)\otimes T_\ell(P)\ar[d]^{\alpha^{(\ell)}_{Q,P}}\\ T_\ell(\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1(P,Q))\ar[r]&T_\ell(\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1(Q,P)), }\]
where the horizontal arrows are the symmetry arrows.

Remark 2.1.14. One can generalize the construction of the homomorphism (2.1.1) and the results 2.1.10 and 2.1.11 concerning it to the case where one is given a commutative Ring \(A\) over \(T\), and two Ideals \(\mathfrak a\) and \(\mathfrak b\) of \(A\) (which in the case treated previously reduce to the same Ideal \(n\mathbb Z_T\) of the constant Ring \(\mathbb Z_T\) over \(T\)). One then defines a homomorphism functorial in the \(A\)-Modules \(P,Q\):

\[(2.1.14.1)\qquad {}_{\mathfrak a}P\otimes{}_{\mathfrak b}Q \mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{\alpha}} \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(\mathfrak a\cap\mathfrak b/\mathfrak a\mathfrak b, \underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1^A(P,Q)),\]
as the composite of the homomorphisms

\[{}_{\mathfrak a}P\otimes{}_{\mathfrak b}Q\longrightarrow \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(A/\mathfrak a,P)\otimes \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(A/\mathfrak b,Q)\longrightarrow \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1(A/\mathfrak a,A/\mathfrak b), \underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1(P,Q)),\]
taking into account the well-known canonical isomorphism
\[(2.1.14.2)\qquad \underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1(A/\mathfrak a,A/\mathfrak b) \xrightarrow{\sim}\mathfrak a\cap\mathfrak b/\mathfrak a\mathfrak b\ ;\]

in fact, it is in the choice of this isomorphism, according as one works with the resolution \(0\longrightarrow\mathfrak a\longrightarrow A\longrightarrow0\) of \(A/\mathfrak a\) or the resolution \(0\longrightarrow\mathfrak b\longrightarrow A\longrightarrow0\) of \(A/\mathfrak b\), that the sign of 2.1.10 is « hidden », which generalizes here to the anticommutativity of the diagram

\[(2.1.14.3)\qquad \xymatrix@C=4.5em@R=3em{ {{}_{\mathfrak a}P\otimes{}_{\mathfrak b}Q}\ar[r]\ar[d]&{{}_{\mathfrak b}Q\otimes{}_{\mathfrak a}P}\ar[d]\\ {\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(\mathfrak a\cap\mathfrak b/\mathfrak a\mathfrak b,\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1(P,Q))}\ar[r]^-{-1}& {\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(\mathfrak a\cap\mathfrak b/\mathfrak a\mathfrak b,\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1(Q,P))}},\]

where the horizontal arrows are the symmetry arrows. When \(\mathfrak a=\mathfrak b\), this anticommutativity can also be expressed by the fact that the symmetry automorphism in \(\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1(A/\mathfrak a,A/\mathfrak a)\) is equal to \(-\mathrm{id}\). In the particular case where \(\mathfrak a=\mathfrak b=nA\), \(n\) being a section of \(A\), then multiplication by \(n\) induces an epimorphism
\[A/\mathfrak a\longrightarrow\mathfrak a\cap\mathfrak a/\mathfrak a\mathfrak a=\mathfrak a/\mathfrak a^2,\]
whence an injective isomorphism functorial in the \(A\)-Module \(T\)
\[\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(\mathfrak a/\mathfrak a^2,T)\longrightarrow{}_{\mathfrak a}T,\]
so that in this case the homomorphism (2.1.14.1) can be interpreted as a homomorphism
\[(2.1.14.4)\qquad {}_nP\otimes{}_nQ\longrightarrow{}_n\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1(P,Q),\]

closer to the form (2.1.1). Assuming still \(\mathfrak a=\mathfrak b=nA\), and \(n\) a regular section of \(A\), the spectral sequence (2.1.4) retains a meaning in the present case, and gives rise to an exact sequence of terms of low degree which generalizes (2.1.5) (\(Q\) being now an \(A\)-Module annihilated by \(\mathfrak a\)):

\[(2.1.14.5)\quad \underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_2^A(P,Q)\longrightarrow \underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_2^{A/nA}(P_n,Q)\longrightarrow {}_nP\otimes Q\mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{\alpha}} \underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1^A(P,Q)\longrightarrow \underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1^{A/nA}(P_n,Q)\longrightarrow0.\]

Let us return to the case where \(\mathfrak a\) and \(\mathfrak b\) are arbitrary, and where one is given two sections \(p\) and \(q\) of \(A\); one then finds commutativity in the following diagram (of which (2.1.11.1) is essentially the particular case obtained by taking \(q=1\)):

\[(2.1.14.6)\qquad \xymatrix@C=4em@R=3em{ &{{}_{p\mathfrak a}P\otimes{}_{q\mathfrak b}Q}\ar[dl]_{p\otimes q}\ar[dr]^{i\otimes j}&\\ {{}_{\mathfrak a}P\otimes{}_{\mathfrak b}Q}\ar[d]_{\alpha}&&{{}_{pq\mathfrak a}P\otimes{}_{pq\mathfrak b}Q}\ar[d]^{\alpha}\\ {\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(\mathfrak a\cap\mathfrak b/\mathfrak a\mathfrak b,\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1(P,Q))}\ar[dr]_{\mathrm{incl.}}&& {\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(pq\mathfrak a\cap pq\mathfrak b/pq\mathfrak a.pq\mathfrak b,\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1(P,Q))}\ar[dl]^{(pq)^*}\\ &{\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(\mathfrak a\cap\mathfrak b/pq\mathfrak a.\mathfrak b,\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1(P,Q))}& }\]

where \(i:{}_{p\mathfrak a}P\longrightarrow{}_{pq\mathfrak a}P\) and \(j:{}_{q\mathfrak b}Q\longrightarrow{}_{pq\mathfrak b}Q\) are the canonical inclusions, and \((pq)^*\) denotes the injective homomorphism deduced by passage to the \(\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}\) from the surjective homomorphism \(\mathfrak a\cap\mathfrak b/pq\mathfrak a.\mathfrak b\longrightarrow pq\mathfrak a\cap\mathfrak b/pq\mathfrak a.pq\mathfrak b\) induced by multiplication by \(pq\). When \(\mathfrak a=\mathfrak b=nA\), this diagram can be rewritten in a simpler form (given in (2.1.11.1) for \(q=1\)).
2.2. Let us now consider, in addition to \(P\) and \(Q\), a commutative Group \(G\). We have then defined in (1.1.3) a canonical homomorphism, functorial in \(P,Q\) and \(G\):
\[(2.2.1)\qquad \operatorname{Biext}^1(P,Q;G)\longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}(\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1(P,Q),G).\]

On the other hand, if \(n\) is an integer \(>0\) (resp. if \(\ell\) is a prime number) then the homomorphism \(\alpha^n_{P,Q}\) of (2.1.1) (resp. the homomorphism \(\alpha^{(\ell)}_{P,Q}\) of (2.1.13.2)) gives a homomorphism
\[\operatorname{Hom}(\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1(P,Q),G)\longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}({}_nP\otimes{}_nQ,G)\]
(resp. a homomorphism
\[\operatorname{Hom}(\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1(P,Q),G)\longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}(T_\ell(P)\otimes T_\ell(Q),T_\ell(G))).\]
These last homomorphisms are likewise functorial in \(P,Q,G\). Composing them with (2.2.1), one deduces canonical homomorphisms, functorial in \(P,Q,G\):

\[(2.2.2)\qquad \varphi^n=\varphi^n_{P,Q}:\operatorname{Biext}^1(P,Q;G)\longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}({}_nP\otimes{}_nQ,G),\] \[(2.2.3)\qquad \varphi^{(\ell)}=\varphi^{(\ell)}_{P,Q}:\operatorname{Biext}^1(P,Q;G)\longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}(T_\ell(P)\otimes T_\ell(Q),T_\ell(G)).\]
For
\[(2.2.4)\qquad \xi\in\operatorname{Biext}^1(P,Q;G),\]
one denotes by \(\varphi^n_\xi\) (resp. \(\varphi^{(\ell)}_\xi\)) its image by (2.2.2) (resp. (2.2.3)):

\[(2.2.5)\qquad \varphi^n_\xi:{}_nP\otimes{}_nQ\longrightarrow G\quad(\text{or }{}_nG,\text{ as desired}),\] \[(2.2.6)\qquad \varphi^{(\ell)}_\xi:T_\ell(P)\otimes T_\ell(Q)\longrightarrow T_\ell(G).\]

2.2.7. Evidently the knowledge of \(\varphi^{(\ell)}_\xi\) is equivalent to that of the \(\varphi^{(n)}_\xi\) for every \(n\) of the form \(\ell^\nu\), with \(\nu\geq0\), and for fixed \(n\), the knowledge of \(\varphi^{(n)}_\xi\) is equivalent to that of the homomorphism
\[(2.2.8)\qquad \underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1(P,Q)\longrightarrow G,\]

image of \(\xi\) by (2.2.1), on the image of \(\alpha^n_{P,Q}:{}_nP\otimes{}_nQ\longrightarrow\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1(P,Q;G)\). One will note that when \(P=nP\) and \(Q=nQ\), then the preceding image is equal to \({}_n\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1(P,Q)\), as follows easily from the consideration of the exact sequence (1.3.5) and of the analogous exact sequence for \(Q\); on the other hand, if \(P\) and \(Q\) are divisible i.e. if \(nP=P,nQ=Q\) for every integer \(n>0\), then one verifies easily that \(\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1(P,Q)\) is torsion i.e.
\[\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1(P,Q)=\varinjlim_n{}_n\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1(P,Q),\]

so that in this case the knowledge of the \(\varphi^n_\xi\) for every \(n\) is equivalent to that of (2.2.8), while the knowledge of \(\varphi^{(\ell)}_\xi\), for a given prime number, is equivalent to that of the restriction of (2.2.8) to the \(\ell\)-primary component of \(\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1(P,Q)\). We shall now make explicit the symmetry properties of the pairings (2.2.4), (2.2.6) associated with biextensions. Let us note first that we have a commutative diagram

\[(2.2.9)\qquad \xymatrix{ {\operatorname{Biext}^1(P,Q;G)}\ar[r]^{\sim}\ar[d]&{\operatorname{Biext}^1(Q,P;G)}\ar[d]\\ {\operatorname{Hom}(\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1(P,Q),G)}\ar[r]^{\sim}& {\operatorname{Hom}(\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1(Q,P),G)}},\]

where the horizontal arrows are the symmetry arrows (the first of these arrows being defined in VII 2.7), and the vertical arrows are (2.2.1). This commutativity follows in fact from the commutativity of the two component squares of the more complete diagram

\[(2.2.10)\qquad \xymatrix{ {\operatorname{Biext}^1(P,Q;G)}\ar[r]^{\sim}\ar[d]&{\operatorname{Biext}^1(Q,P;G)}\ar[d]\\ {\operatorname{Ext}^1(P\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathrm L}}Q,G)}\ar[r]^{\sim}\ar[d]& {\operatorname{Ext}^1(Q\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathrm L}}P,G)}\ar[d]\\ {\operatorname{Hom}(\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1(P,Q),G)}\ar[r]^{\sim}& {\operatorname{Hom}(\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1(Q,P),G)}},\]

where the first vertical arrow of each column is the canonical isomorphism of VII 3.6.5, and the second arrow is the obvious homomorphism. The commutativity of the second square of (2.2.10) is trivial, taking into account that the symmetry isomorphism \(P\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathrm L}}Q\xrightarrow{\sim}Q\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathrm L}}P\) induces by definition the symmetry isomorphism \(\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1(P,Q)\simeq\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1(Q,P)\), and that for a variable complex \(L\), the canonical homomorphism \(\operatorname{Ext}^1(L,G)\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Hom}(H_1(L),G)\) is functorial in \(L\). As for the commutativity of the first square of (2.2.10), it follows from the simultaneous explicitation of the symmetry isomorphisms and of the vertical isomorphisms defined in VII 3.6.5, and we leave the detail of the verification to the reader; one will use the fact that the symmetry isomorphism \(L_\bullet(P)\otimes L_\bullet(Q)\simeq L_\bullet(Q)\otimes L_\bullet(P)\) induces in degree 1 the isomorphism
\[L_0(P)\otimes L_1(Q)+L_1(P)\otimes L_0(Q)\xrightarrow{\sim} L_0(Q)\otimes L_1(P)+L_1(Q)\otimes L_0(P)\]
which is given on the corresponding summands by the ordinary symmetry isomorphism of the underlying non-graded modules (hence without the intervention of a sign).
Taking into account the commutativity of (2.2.9), the anticommutativity of (2.1.10.1) is translated, in terms of the \(\varphi^n\) or of the \(\varphi^{(\ell)}\), into the following relation:

blocks.title: Proposition 2.2.11.
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.2.11. We have anticommutativity in the following diagrams :
\[(2.2.11.1)\qquad \xymatrix{ \operatorname{Biext}^1(P,Q;G)\ar[r]^{\sim}\ar[d]_{\varphi^n_{P,Q}}& \operatorname{Biext}^1(Q,P;G)\ar[d]^{\varphi^n_{Q,P}}\\ \operatorname{Hom}({}_nP\otimes{}_nQ,G)\ar[r]^{\sim}& \operatorname{Hom}({}_nQ\otimes{}_nP,G), }\] \[(2.2.11.2)\qquad \xymatrix{ \operatorname{Biext}^1(P,Q;G)\ar[r]^{\sim}\ar[d]_{\varphi^{(\ell)}_{P,Q}}& \operatorname{Biext}^1(Q,P;G)\ar[d]^{\varphi^{(\ell)}_{Q,P}}\\ \operatorname{Hom}(T_\ell(P)\otimes T_\ell(Q),T_\ell(G))\ar[r]^{\sim}& \operatorname{Hom}(T_\ell(Q)\otimes T_\ell(P),T_\ell(G)), }\]

where the horizontal arrows are the symmetry arrows.
Let us point out the important corollary:

blocks.title: Corollary 2.2.12.
blocks.html: Corollary 2.2.12. Suppose \(P=Q\), and suppose that \(\xi\) is the class of a symmetric (resp. antisymmetric) biextension of \((P,Q)\) by \(G\), i.e. that we have \({}^s\xi=\xi\) (resp. \({}^s\xi=-\xi\)), where \({}^s\xi\) denotes the image of \(\xi\) by symmetry. Then the bilinear maps \(\varphi^n_\xi\) (on \({}_nP\times{}_nP\)) and \(\varphi^{(\ell)}_\xi\) (on \(T_\ell(P)\times T_\ell(P)\)) are antisymmetric (resp. symmetric).

Remark 2.2.13. Suppose \(\xi\) symmetric. If \(n\) (resp. \(\ell\)) is odd, then it follows from 2.2.12 that \(\varphi^n_\xi\) (resp. \(\varphi^{(\ell)}_\xi\)) is even an alternating form. This conclusion is not valid in general without the restriction \(n\) resp. \(\ell\) odd, as one sees already in the case where \(P\) is the ordinary group \(\mathbb Z/2\mathbb Z\), and \(G=\mathbb Z/2\mathbb Z\). Suppose however that we have \(2P=P\), (which is the case in particular if \(P\) is an abelian scheme, working as usual with the fppf (or fpqc) topology), then it is still true that the preceding forms are alternating. It suffices to see that \(\varphi^{2^\nu}(x,x)=0\); now, as \(2P=P\), one may assume that the given element \(x\in{}_{2^\nu}P(S)\) is of the form \(2y\), with \(y\in{}_{2^{\nu+1}}P(S)\), and one then has
\[\varphi^{2^\nu}(x,x)=2\varphi^{2^{\nu+1}}(y,y)=0,\quad\text{since }\varphi^{2^{\nu+1}}\text{ is antisymmetric}.\]
2.3. Relation with « Cartier duality ».

The description given in 2.2 of the pairings defined by a biextension, via the isomorphism theorem VII 3.6.5 and the homomorphism (2.2.1), is evidently not the definition that had been used previously in the case of abelian schemes (at a time when the very notion of biextension did not yet exist!). The latter proceeds via Cartier duality, and we shall now give the relation with the latter.
Let us take up again the homomorphism
\[(2.3.1)\qquad \operatorname{Biext}^1(P,Q;G)\longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}(P,\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^1(Q,G))\]
coming from the exact sequence (1.4), which is moreover an isomorphism in the case where we have
\[(2.3.2)\qquad \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(Q,G)=0,\]

a condition satisfied in particular when \(Q\) is an abelian scheme over a scheme \(S\), and \(G\) is the multiplicative group \(G_m\) (VII 1.3.8). We have on the other hand an obvious homomorphism « restriction to \({}_nQ\) », \(\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^1(Q,G)\longrightarrow\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^1({}_nQ,G)\), whence a composite homomorphism

\[(2.3.4)\qquad \operatorname{Hom}(P,\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^1(Q,G))\longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}(P,\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^1({}_nQ,G))\longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}({}_nP,{}_n\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^1({}_nQ,G)),\]
where the second arrow is the homomorphism of restriction to \({}_nP\). Composing with (2.3.1), one finds a homomorphism
\[(2.3.5)\qquad \operatorname{Biext}^1(P,Q;G)\longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}({}_nP,{}_n\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^1({}_nQ,G)).\]
On the other hand, the exact sequence
\[(2.3.6)\qquad 0\longrightarrow{}_nQ\longrightarrow Q\xrightarrow n nQ\longrightarrow0\]
gives rise to an exact sequence of the \(\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^i(-,G)\), whence a short exact sequence

\[(2.3.7)\qquad 0\longrightarrow\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(Q,G)_n\longrightarrow \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}({}_nQ,G)\xrightarrow\partial {}_n\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^1(nQ,G)\longrightarrow0,\]
where \(E_n\) denotes, as usual, \(E/nE\). From it we shall retain the canonical surjective homomorphism
\[(2.3.8)\qquad \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}({}_nQ,G)\xrightarrow\partial {}_n\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^1(nQ,G),\]
which is moreover an isomorphism if \(\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(Q,G)_n=0\), a fortiori if we have (2.3.2). From (2.3.8) one deduces a homomorphism

\[(2.3.9)\qquad \operatorname{Hom}({}_nP,\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}({}_nQ,G))\simeq \operatorname{Hom}({}_nP\otimes{}_nQ,G)\longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}({}_nP,{}_n\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^1(nQ,G)),\]
which is an isomorphism if we have (2.3.2).
All the homomorphisms under consideration are functorial in \(P,Q,G\).
The homomorphisms (2.3.5), (2.3.9) and (2.2.2) give rise to a diagram

\[(2.3.10)\qquad \begin{array}{c} \operatorname{Biext}^1(P,Q;G)\\[-2pt] \swarrow\,\scriptstyle\varphi^n\qquad\searrow\,\scriptstyle(2.3.5)\\[-2pt] \operatorname{Hom}({}_nP\otimes{}_nQ,G)\xrightarrow{\ (2.3.9)\ } \operatorname{Hom}({}_nP,{}_n\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^1(nQ,G)), \end{array}\]
where the horizontal arrow (2.3.9) is an isomorphism if we have (2.3.2).

blocks.title: Proposition 2.3.11.
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.3.11.

The diagram (2.3.10) is anticommutative.

In the case where \(\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(Q,G)=0\), this statement thus gives back a characterization of the homomorphism \(\varphi^n\) of (2.2.2) in terms of the homomorphisms (2.3.5) and (2.3.9), which is the one used previously in the theory of abelian schemes (where moreover we have \(nQ=Q\)).

Proof of 2.3.11 (*). Let \(\xi\) be an element of \(\operatorname{Biext}^1(P,Q;G)\), \(\varphi_\xi:{}_nP\otimes{}_nQ\longrightarrow G\) the associated pairing, and \(u_\xi:{}_nP\longrightarrow\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^1(nQ,G)\) the homomorphism image of \(\xi\) by (2.3.5). One must prove that \(-u_\xi\) is deduced from \(\varphi_\xi\) by the arrow (2.3.9), i.e. that it is the composite
\[(*)\qquad {}_nP\xrightarrow{\ \varphi_\xi\ } \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}({}_nQ,G)\xrightarrow{\ (2.3.8)\ } {}_n\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^1(nQ,G).\]

To prove this, one may suppose given a section \(x\) of \({}_nP\) and prove that its image \(-u_\xi\) is equal to its image by the composite (*). (This would allow us, if we wished, to reduce to the case where \(P=(\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z)_T\), but this reduction will not be necessary for us.)
a) Let us choose flat resolutions \(L_\bullet\) and \(M_\bullet\),
\[0\longrightarrow L_1\longrightarrow L_0\longrightarrow0, \qquad 0\longrightarrow M_1\longrightarrow M_0\longrightarrow0\]

of \(P\) and \(Q\) respectively, a flat resolution of \(P\) (for example) being obtained by writing \(P\) as a quotient of a flat \(\mathbb Z_T\)-Module \(L_0\), and by denoting by \(L_1\) the kernel of \(L_0\longrightarrow P\) (which is flat, being torsion-free). From this one deduces a complex \(L_\bullet\otimes M_\bullet\)
\[0\longrightarrow L_1\otimes M_1\xrightarrow{d_2} L_1\otimes M_0+L_0\otimes M_1\xrightarrow{d_1}L_0\otimes M_0\longrightarrow0,\]

whose sheaf \(\underline H_1\) will serve us as \(\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1(P,Q)\). Let us denote by \(L'_0\) resp. \(M'_0\) the subsheaf of \(L_0\) resp. \(M_0\) inverse image of \({}_nP\) resp. \({}_nQ\), so that the complex \(0\longrightarrow L_1\longrightarrow L'_0\longrightarrow0\) (resp. the complex \(0\longrightarrow M_1\longrightarrow M'_0\longrightarrow0\)) is a flat resolution of \({}_nP\) (resp. of \({}_nQ\)). One can then make explicit the homomorphism
\[{}_nP\otimes{}_nQ\longrightarrow\underline{\operatorname{Tor}}_1(P,Q) =\underline H_1(L_\bullet\otimes M_\bullet)\]
by passage to the quotient starting from the homomorphism
\[L'_0\otimes M'_0\longrightarrow\underline H_1(L_\bullet\otimes M_\bullet)\]
given by
\[x_0\otimes y_0\longmapsto\text{class of the 1-cycle } [nx_0]\otimes y_0-x_0\otimes[ny_0],\]

where the brackets \([\ ]\) indicate that the element under consideration is considered as a section of the subsheaf \(L_1\) of \(L_0\) resp. \(M_1\) of \(M_0\). The preceding explicitation follows in fact from the computation made in the proof of 2.1.10.
b) Let us take an injective resolution
\[0\longrightarrow C^0\xrightarrow{d^0}C^1\longrightarrow\cdots\]

of \(G\), and let us identify \(\operatorname{Biext}^1(P,Q;G)\) with \(\operatorname{Ext}^1(P\mathbin{\overset{\mathbb L}{\otimes}}Q,G) =H^1(\operatorname{Hom}(L_\bullet\otimes M_\bullet,C^\bullet))\). Hence the element \(\xi\) can be interpreted as a pair \(f_1,f_0\) of homomorphisms, giving rise to an anticommutative diagram
\[(2.3.12)\qquad \begin{array}{ccc} L_1\otimes M_0+L_0\otimes M_1&\xrightarrow{d_1}&L_0\otimes M_0\\ \scriptstyle f_1\downarrow&&\downarrow\scriptstyle f_0\\ C^0&\xrightarrow{d^0}&C^1. \end{array}\]
By definition, the pairing
\[\varphi_\xi:{}_nP\otimes{}_nQ\longrightarrow G\]
is then deduced by passage to the quotient from the homomorphism
\[\varphi:L'_0\otimes M'_0\longrightarrow G\]
given by
\[(2.3.13)\qquad \varphi(x_0\otimes y_0)= f_1([nx_0]\otimes y_0-x_0\otimes[ny_0])\in G=\operatorname{Ker}(C^0\longrightarrow C^1).\]

We may assume that the given section \(x\) of \({}_nP\) lifts to a section \(x_0\) of \(L'_0\) (for the assertion (2.3.11) is local on \(T\)), and the preceding formula, where \(y_0\) is considered as variable, thus defines a homomorphism
\[(2.3.14)\qquad \varphi'_0:M'_0\longrightarrow G,\qquad y_0\longmapsto f_1([nx_0]\otimes y_0-x_0\otimes[ny_0]),\]
which passes to the quotient to give us the homomorphism
\[{}_nQ\longrightarrow G,\qquad y\longmapsto\varphi_\xi(x,y)\]
associated with \(\varphi_\xi\) and with \(x\).

c) One must take the image of this homomorphism by the operator \(\partial\) of (2.3.7), associated with the exact sequence (2.3.6). Now \(nQ\simeq Q/{}_nQ\) is evidently also isomorphic to \(M_0/M'_0\), so that we have a homomorphism of exact sequences

\[\begin{array}{ccccccccc} 0&\longrightarrow&M'_0&\longrightarrow&M_0&\longrightarrow&M_0/M'_0&\longrightarrow&0\\ &&\downarrow&&\downarrow&&\wr\downarrow\\ 0&\longrightarrow&{}_nQ&\longrightarrow&Q&\longrightarrow&nQ&\longrightarrow&0, \end{array}\]

where the last vertical arrow is an isomorphism. This shows that the sought section of \(\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^1(nQ,G)\) can also be computed as the image of \(\varphi'\) (2.3.14) by the coboundary operator
\[\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(M'_0,G)\xrightarrow{\partial} \underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^1(M_0/M'_0,G),\]
or again as the section of the second member defined by the element of the global \(\operatorname{Ext}^1\), image of \(\varphi'\) by the coboundary operator
\[\operatorname{Hom}(M'_0,G)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Ext}^1(M_0/M'_0,G).\]
The usual construction of the coboundary operator then gives us the following recipe: one prolongs the composite (2.3.13)
\[M'_0\xrightarrow{\ \varphi'\ }G\longrightarrow C^0\]
to a homomorphism
\[\psi:M_0\longrightarrow C^0,\]
which is possible since \(C^0\) is injective, and one considers the unique homomorphism
\[(2.3.15)\qquad \lambda:M_0/M'_0\longrightarrow C^1\]
making commutative the diagram

\[\begin{array}{ccccccccc} 0&\longrightarrow&M'_0&\longrightarrow&M_0&\longrightarrow&M_0/M'_0&\longrightarrow&0\\ &&\scriptstyle\varphi'\downarrow&&\scriptstyle\psi\downarrow&&\scriptstyle\lambda\downarrow\\ 0&\longrightarrow&G&\longrightarrow&C^0&\xrightarrow{d^0}&C^1. \end{array}\]

It is clear that (2.3.15) takes its values in the 1-cocycles, i.e. is a 1-cocycle, and that the latter represents the sought element of \(\operatorname{Ext}^1(nQ,G)=\operatorname{Ext}^1(M_0/M'_0,G)\).

d) It remains to make explicit the section of \(\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^1(nQ,G)\) deduced from \(\xi\) and from \(x\) thanks to the arrow (2.3.5) of the diagram (2.3.10). For this, we may ignore the fact that the section \(x\) of \(P\) is annihilated by \(n\), and one finds the following description, in terms of the chosen lifting \(x_0\) of \(x\) to a section of \(L_0\): the latter defines an augmentation homomorphism
\[u:M_\bullet\longrightarrow L_\bullet\otimes M_\bullet,\qquad y_0\longmapsto x_0\otimes y_0,\quad y_1\longmapsto x_0\otimes y_1,\]

and the section of \(\operatorname{Ext}^1(Q,G)\) deduced from (2.3.1) is the one which is deduced from the element \(\xi\) of \(\operatorname{Ext}^1(L_\bullet\otimes M_\bullet,G)\) by composing with the preceding homomorphism, hence is given by the homomorphism of degree 1

\[\begin{array}{ccccc} 0&\longrightarrow&M_1&\xrightarrow{d_1}&M_0&\longrightarrow0\\ &&\scriptstyle y_1\mapsto f_1(x_0\otimes y_1)\downarrow&& \downarrow\scriptstyle y_0\mapsto f_0(x_0\otimes y_0)\\ 0&\longrightarrow&C^0&\xrightarrow{d^0}&C^1&\longrightarrow\cdots . \end{array}\]
To take the restriction of this class to \(nQ\lhookrightarrow Q\), let us use the isomorphism \(nQ\simeq M_0/M'_0\), which furnishes a flat resolution
\[0\longrightarrow M'_0\longrightarrow M_0\longrightarrow0\]
of \(nQ\), the inclusion \(nQ\lhookrightarrow Q\) being lifted to a homomorphism of resolutions

\[\begin{array}{ccccc} 0&\longrightarrow&M'_0&\longrightarrow&M_0&\longrightarrow0\\ &&\scriptstyle y_0\mapsto[ny_0]\downarrow&& \downarrow\scriptstyle y_0\mapsto ny_0\\[-2pt] 0&\longrightarrow&M_1&\longrightarrow&M_0&\longrightarrow0. \end{array}\]

By composition, one thus sees that the sought section of \(\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^1(nQ,G)\) is associated with the element of \(\operatorname{Ext}^1(nQ,G)\) represented by the homomorphism of complexes of degree 1

\[\begin{array}{ccccc} 0&\longrightarrow&M'_0&\xrightarrow{d_1}&M_0&\longrightarrow0\\ &&\scriptstyle g_1\downarrow&&\downarrow\scriptstyle g_0\\ 0&\longrightarrow&C^0&\longrightarrow&C^1&\longrightarrow\cdots, \end{array}\]
with

e) Using now the fact that \(x\) is a section of \({}_nP\), let us show that the preceding element of \(\operatorname{Ext}^1(nQ,G)\) is the opposite of the one defined by (2.3.15). For this, let us note that for every homomorphism
\[h:M_0\longrightarrow C^0,\]
the element of the \(\operatorname{Ext}^1\) considered in d) does not change if one changes \(g_1,g_0\) respectively into
Now we have at our disposal a homomorphism \(\psi:M_0\longrightarrow C^0\) constructed in c), which by definition satisfies the condition
\[\psi(y'_0)=f_1([nx_0]\otimes y'_0-x_0\otimes[ny'_0])\]
for \(y'_0\) a section of \(M'_0\), which can also be written
let us therefore define \(h:M_0\longrightarrow C^0\) by
hence by construction we shall have
now we have \(d^0f_1=-f_0d_1\) hence \(d^0f_1([nx_0]\otimes y_0)=-f_0(nx\otimes y_0)\), and there remains

so that the element under consideration is the one defined by the homomorphism \(M_0/M'_0\xrightarrow{\sim}nQ\longrightarrow C^1\) deduced from \(-d^0\psi\) by passage to the quotient. One thus finds the opposite of the element defined by (2.3.15).

Remarks 2.3.16. The preceding proof gives in fact a compatibility slightly more precise than the one indicated in 2.3.11, obtained by choosing a section \(x\) of \({}_nP\). This section then defines a homomorphism \(y\longmapsto\varphi_\xi(x,y)\) of \({}_nQ\) into \(G\), whence by the coboundary homomorphism relative to (2.3.6) an element of \(\operatorname{Ext}^1(nQ,G)\). On the other hand, forgetting that \(x\) is annihilated by \(n\), one considers the homomorphism \(\mathbb Z_T\longrightarrow P\) defined by \(x\), whence by inverse image a biextension of \((\mathbb Z_T,Q)\) by \(G\), whose class can be interpreted as an element of \(\operatorname{Ext}^1(Q,G)\), and thus induces an element of \(\operatorname{Ext}^1(nQ,G)\). This being so, the two elements obtained in \(\operatorname{Ext}^1(nQ,G)\) are opposite.

2.3.17. Let us take advantage of the explicitation in terms of cocycles of the pairing \(\varphi_\xi^n:{}_nP\otimes{}_nQ\longrightarrow G\), given in (2.3.12), to give another expression of this pairing. Suppose first that the biextension \(E\) with class \(\xi\) admits a section \(s\) over \(P\times Q\). Let us take, as in VII 3.8.13, as flat resolution of \(P\) the one given by \(L_0=\mathbb Z[P]\), \(L_1\) being the kernel of the canonical homomorphism \(\mathbb Z[P]\longrightarrow P\), and let us define \(M_\bullet\) likewise. Then there exists a unique trivialization of biextension of the inverse image of \(E\) over \((L_0,M_0)\), whose restriction to \(P\times Q\) is given by \(s\); let us again call it \(s\) (cf. VII 3.8.2). By virtue of VII 3.8.12, the class of \(\xi\) in \(\operatorname{Ext}^1(L_\bullet\otimes M_\bullet,G)\) is given by the cocycle with components \((f,g)\), characterized by
\[s(x_1,y_0)=f(x_1\otimes y_0)e_{E/Q}(y_0),\qquad s(x_0,y_1)=g(x_0\otimes y_1)e_{E/P}(x_0).\]

If then \(x,y\) are sections of \({}_nP\) and \({}_nQ\), one lifts them to the corresponding sections \(x_0=[x]\), \(y_0=[y]\) of \(L_0\) and \(M_0\), and one applies the formula (2.3.13), which gives us, setting \(z=s(x,y)\):

where the exponent \(n\) on the left resp. on the right of \(z\) indicates the \(n\)-th power for the left resp. right multiplicative structure on \(E\), so that one finds a section of \(E\) above \((0,y)\) resp. \((x,0)\); the meaning of the « quotient » of such an element by \(e_{E/Q}(y)\) resp. \(e_{E/P}(x)\) is clear.

Let us note now that the preceding formula (2.3.18) retains a meaning, as soon as \(z\) is a global section of \(E\) which lifts the sections \(x,y\) of \({}_nP,{}_nQ\). I claim that the formula (2.3.18) is valid for every such section, without assuming that there exists a section of \(E\) over \(P\times Q\). As the section \((x,y)\) of \(P\times Q\) lifts in any case locally to a section of \(E\) (since \(E\longrightarrow P\times Q\) is an epimorphism), this therefore makes it possible to compute \(\varphi_\xi^n(x,y)\) in all cases. Note moreover that if one replaces \(z\) by another section \(z'\) of \(E\) lifting \((x,y)\), one will have \(z'=z+g\), where \(g\) is a section of \(G\), and the two terms of the second member of (2.3.18) corresponding to \(z'\) are those corresponding to \(z\), augmented by \(ng\), so that the difference is indeed independent of \(z\), which shows us that the formula (2.3.18) does indeed define a well-determined homomorphism \({}_nP\otimes{}_nQ\longrightarrow G\).

It remains to show that the latter is indeed \(\varphi^n\). Now for this we are reduced at once to the case where \(P=Q=(\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z)_T\), so that \(P\times Q\) is of the form \(I_T\), where \(I=\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z\times\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z\) is a finite set. In this case, the torsor \(E\) under \(G_{P\times Q}\) (which is identified with a family of torsors \(E_i\) under \(G\), \(i\in I\)) evidently becomes trivial locally on \(T\), so that we are reduced to the case where there exists a global section of \(E\) over \(P\times Q\), a case already treated.
2.3.19. Another proof of 2.3.11 (by DELIGNE).
a) Reduction to \(P=\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z\), and to \(T=(\mathrm{Ens})\).
b) Let \(\partial\) be the following arrow from \({}_nQ\) into \({}_nQ[1]\):
\[{}_nQ\xleftarrow[H^0]{\sim}[Q\xrightarrow{n}Q]\longrightarrow{}_nQ[1].\]
The following diagram is anticommutative:
\[\begin{array}{ccc} {}_nQ[0]&\xrightarrow{1}&[Q\xrightarrow{n}Q]\\ \scriptstyle\partial\downarrow&&\nearrow\scriptstyle 1\\ {}_nQ[1]&& \end{array}\]
Indeed, the arrows

\[\begin{array}{ccccc} [{}_nQ&\longrightarrow&Q]&&[{}_nQ&\longrightarrow&Q]\\ \scriptstyle 0\downarrow&&\downarrow\scriptstyle n&& \scriptstyle 1\downarrow&&\downarrow\scriptstyle 0\\ [Q&\xrightarrow{n}&Q]&&[Q&\xrightarrow{n}&Q] \end{array}\]
are opposite thanks to
\[\begin{array}{ccc} [{}_nQ\longrightarrow Q]\\[-2pt] &\searrow\scriptstyle 1\\ \scriptstyle H\downarrow&&[Q\xrightarrow{n}Q]. \end{array}\]
c) 2.3.10 is the diagram

\[ \begin{CD} H^1\mathrm{RHom}(\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z\mathbin{\mathop\otimes^L}Q,G) @>>> H^1\mathrm{RHom}(\mathbb Z\mathbin{\mathop\otimes^L}Q,G)\\ @VVV @VVV @. @. @VV{+}V\\ \operatorname{Hom}(\operatorname{Tor}_1(\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z,Q),G) @>>> \operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z,\operatorname{Ext}^1(Q,G)) @. @. \operatorname{Ext}^1(Q,G)\\ @VVV @VVV @. @. @VVV\\ \operatorname{Hom}({}_nQ,G) @>>> \operatorname{Ext}^1(nQ,G), \end{CD}\]
and
c 1) \(\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z\mathbin{\mathop\otimes^L}Q\simeq [Q\xrightarrow{n}Q]\), and, modulo this isomorphism, the 1st composite vertical arrow is induced by
\[{}_nQ[1]\longrightarrow[Q\xrightarrow{n}Q];\]
c 2) the 2nd horizontal arrow is the transpose of the arrow \(\partial\) indicated above in b);
c 3) if one runs through the diagram by the other path, it is clear that one lands on the arrow induced by \({}_nQ[0]\longrightarrow[Q\xrightarrow{n}Q]\). Whence the conclusion 2.3.11.
\[f:T'\longrightarrow T\]
be a morphism of topoi. Then we have a canonical homomorphism (VII 2.8)
\[\operatorname{Biext}^1(P,Q;G)\longrightarrow \operatorname{Biext}^1(P',Q';G'),\]
where the ′ denote the inverse image Groups. As, for every commutative Group \(E\) of \(T\), we evidently have a canonical isomorphism
\[({}_nE)'\mathrel{\mathop\simeq}{}_n(E'),\]
the homomorphisms (2.2.2) on \(T\) and on \(T'\) make it possible to define a canonical diagram

\[(2.4.1)\qquad \begin{CD} \operatorname{Biext}^1(P,Q;G) @>>> \operatorname{Biext}^1(P',Q';G')\\ @V{\varphi^n_{P,Q}}VV @VV{\varphi^n_{P',Q'}}V\\ \operatorname{Hom}({}_nP\otimes{}_nQ,G) @>>> \operatorname{Hom}({}_nP'\otimes{}_nQ',G'). \end{CD}\]

Similarly, the homomorphisms (2.2.3) give rise to an analogous diagram in terms of the \(T_\ell\). This being so, I claim that the diagrams under consideration are commutative. The verification is essentially trivial in terms of the definitions, and left to the reader. One will note moreover, more generally, that all the canonical homomorphisms considered in the present section and the preceding one are compatible with change of topos, in an obvious sense. We shall use in the sequel without further mention the compatibilities of this nature.
