pages.title: 3. Biextensions of group schemes \((P,Q)\) by \(G_m\): generalities

blocks.html: 3. Biextensions of group schemes \((P,Q)\) by \(G_m\): generalities
In the present section, we fix a base scheme \(S\), and, as announced in n° 0, we work in the topos \(fppf\) of \(S\).

3.1. For the record, let us first recall the most important example of a group \(\operatorname{Biext}^1(P,Q;G_m)\): the one where \(P\) and \(Q\) are two abelian schemes over \(S\), and where the group \(\operatorname{Biext}^1\) is interpreted as the group of divisorial correspondences on \((P,Q)\) (VII 2.9.4). The \(\ell\)-adic pairings of the preceding section are then the well-known pairings [1] in the theory of abelian schemes.
3.2. More generally, let us suppose only that \(Q\) is an abelian scheme; then it is well known (VII 1.3.8) that we have
\[(3.2.1)\qquad \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(Q,G_m)=0,\]
hence (1.4) we have a canonical isomorphism
\[(3.2.2)\qquad \operatorname{Biext}^1(P,Q;G_m)\mathrel{\mathop\simeq} \operatorname{Hom}(P,Q^*),\]
where
\[(3.2.3)\qquad Q^*=\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^1(Q,G_m)\]
is the dual abelian scheme of \(Q\). (For the representability of \(Q^*\), due to M. RAYNAUD in the general case, cf. [1].)
When \(P\) is likewise an abelian scheme, we have, symmetrically to (3.2.2), a canonical isomorphism
\[(3.2.4)\qquad \operatorname{Biext}^1(P,Q;G_m)\mathrel{\mathop\simeq} \operatorname{Hom}(Q,P^*).\]
Thus the datum of a biextension \(\xi\) of \((P,Q)\) by \(G_m\) can be interpreted, indifferently, by the datum of a homomorphism
\[u:P\longrightarrow Q^*\]
or of a homomorphism
\[v:Q\longrightarrow P^*.\]

Moreover, one knows that a homomorphism of abelian schemes \(w:A\longrightarrow B\) is an isomorphism if and only if for every integer \(n>0\), the induced homo- morphism \({}_nw:{}_nA\longrightarrow{}_nB\) is an isomorphism. Now in the case of \(u\) and of \(v\) above, \({}_nu:{}_nP\longrightarrow{}_nQ^*\) is identified with the homomorphism \({}_nP\longrightarrow D({}_nQ)\) defined by the pairing \(\varphi^n_\xi:{}_nP\times{}_nQ\longrightarrow G_m\) (cf. 2.3.11), while \({}_nv:{}_nQ\longrightarrow{}_nP^*\) is identified with the homomorphism \({}_nQ\longrightarrow D({}_nP)\) defined by the pairing \(\psi^n_\xi:{}_nP\times{}_nQ\longrightarrow G_m\). Now we have \(\varphi^n_\xi=-\psi^n_\xi\) (2.1.10), hence up to sign, \({}_nu\) and \({}_nv\) are transposes of one another. Taking into account the well-known fact that the \({}_nP\) and the \({}_nQ\) are finite and locally free over \(S\), hence satisfy Cartier duality, one thus finds that \({}_nu\) is an isomorphism if and only if \({}_nv\) is an isomorphism, hence that \(u\) is an isomorphism if and only if \(v\) is an isomorphism.

An equivalent (slightly less symmetric) way of stating this result is the following: consider the canonical biextension (Weil biextension) of \((P,P^*)\) by \(G_m\) (corresponding to \(v:P^*\longrightarrow P^*\) the identity); then the corresponding canonical homomorphism \(u:P\longrightarrow P^{**}\) is an isomorphism. This is the biduality theorem of BARSOTTI and CARTIER.
3.3. We pass to the case where \(Q\) is finite locally free over \(S\), or is a scheme of multiplicative type over \(S\). The key lemma in this case is the following (well-known) result:

Proposition 3.3.1.

Let \(Q\) be a commutative group scheme over \(S\), and suppose that \(Q\) is finite locally free over \(S\), or that \(Q\) is of finite type and of multiplicative type over \(S\) (SGA 3 IX). Then we have

\[\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^1(Q,G_m)=0.\]
Proof. Let us distinguish the two cases under consideration.

a) \(Q\) is finite locally free over \(S\). One must prove that every extension \(E\) of \(Q\) by \(G_m\) splits locally for the topology \(fppf\). One may evidently suppose \(S\) affine; there then exists an integer \(n>0\) such that we have \(nQ=0\) (SGA 3 IX 6.1). On the other hand, one knows that \(n\operatorname{id}\) is an epimorphism in \(G_m\) for \(fppf\), hence the « Kummer » exact sequence
\[0\longrightarrow\mu_n\longrightarrow G_m\mathrel{\mathop\longrightarrow^n}G_m\longrightarrow0\]
gives rise to an exact sequence of the \(\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^i(Q,-)\), which gives us an epimorphism
\[\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^1(Q,\mu_n)\longrightarrow {}_n\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^1(Q,G_m)=\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^1(Q,G_m),\]

the last equality coming from the fact that multiplication by \(n\), being null in \(Q\), is likewise null in \(\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^1(Q,G_m)\). This shows that the extension \(E\) comes from an extension \(F\) of \(Q\) by \(\mu_n\); \(F\) is represen- table by a finite locally free scheme over \(S\) (fpqc descent theory, SGA 1 VIII). Because of the known properties of Cartier duality of finite locally free groups (SGA 3 VII 3), the dual \(D(F)=\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(F,G_m)\) is an extension of \(D(\mu_n)\) by \(D(Q)\). (One must see that if \(A\longrightarrow B\) is a monomorphism of finite locally free commutative groups over \(S\), then \(D(B)\longrightarrow D(A)\) is a faithfully flat morphism. As \(D(B)\) and \(D(A)\) are likewise finite locally free over \(S\), the fiberwise criterion of faithful flatness applies and reduces us to the case where \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\). In this case, our assertion means simply that the homomorphism on the affine rings \(k(D(A))\longrightarrow k(D(B))\) is injective (SGA 3 VIB 11.14); now this homomorphism is the transpose of the homomorphism \(k(B)\longrightarrow k(A)\), which is surjective since \(A\longrightarrow B\) is a closed immersion (SGA 3 VIB 1.4.2). Hence \(k(D(A))\longrightarrow k(D(B))\) is indeed injective, qed.) Now the inclusion \(\mu_n\longrightarrow G_m\) is identified with a section of \(D(\mu_n)=\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(\mu_n,G_m)\), and everything comes down to proving that this inclusion extends, locally \(fppf\) on \(S\), to a homomorphism \(F\longrightarrow G_m\), i.e. that the section under consideration of \(D(\mu_n)\) lifts locally \(fppf\) to a section of \(D(F)\). Now this is clear, since \(D(F)\longrightarrow D(\mu_n)\) is faithfully flat and of finite presentation.

b) By virtue of (SGA 3 X 4.5), after localizing on \(S\) for the étale topology, we may assume that \(Q\) is diagonalizable, i.e. of the form \(D(M)\), where \(M\) is a \(\mathbb Z\)-module of finite type. Let \(T\) be the torsion submodule of \(M\), and \(L=M/T\) the quotient, which is isomorphic to \(\mathbb Z^r\).
Then the exact sequence \(0\longrightarrow T\longrightarrow M\longrightarrow L\longrightarrow0\) gives an exact sequence
\[0\longrightarrow D(L)\longrightarrow D(M)\longrightarrow D(T)\longrightarrow0\quad\text{(SGA 3 IX 3.1)},\]

hence we are reduced to proving separately that the \(\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^1\) of \(D(L)\) and of \(D(T)\) with \(G_m\) is null. The case of \(D(T)\) falls under a), the case of \(D(L)\) reduces to the one where \(L=\mathbb Z\), so that we are reduced to verifying that we have
\[\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^1(G_m,G_m)=0,\]

i.e. that every extension \(E\) of \(G_m\) by \(G_m\) is locally trivial fppf. Now such an extension is representable by a group scheme affine and flat over \(S\) (fpqc descent theory SGA 1 VIII), whose fibers are tori, hence it is a torus (SGA 3 X 4.9 and ). By virtue of SGA 3 X 4.5, we may assume that \(E\) is a trivial torus \(D(R)\simeq G_m^2\). Moreover, after localizing in the Zariski sense, we may assume that the monomorphism \(G_m\longrightarrow E=D(R)\) comes from a constant morphism \(R\longrightarrow\mathbb Z_S\) (SGA 3 VIII 1.4), and the latter is necessarily an epimorphism (SGA 3 VIII 3.2 b)). Choosing an \(r\in R\) above the element 1 of \(\mathbb Z\), one thus finds a homomorphism \(E\longrightarrow G_m\) which splits the extension \(E\), which proves the desired assertion.

Corollary 3.3.2.

Let \(Q\) be as in 3.3.1. Then we have, for every \(P\), a canonical isomorphism

\[(3.3.2.1)\qquad \operatorname{Biext}^1(P,Q;G_m)\xleftarrow{\sim} \operatorname{Ext}^1(P,D(Q)),\]

where \(D(Q)\) denotes the Cartier dual \(\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(Q,G_m)\) of \(Q\). More precisely, the canonical functor (1.1.6) is an equivalence of the category of extensions of \(P\) by \(D(Q)\) with the category of biextensions of \((P,Q)\) by \(G_m\).
This is in fact a particular case of 1.5, taking 3.3.1 into account.

Remarks 3.3.3. Under the conditions of 3.3.1, \(D(Q)\) is representable by a group scheme separated, locally of finite presentation and locally quasi-finite over \(S\). More precisely, if \(Q\) is finite locally free over \(S\), the same is true of \(D(Q)\) as is well known (SGA 3 VII 3), and if \(Q\) is of multiplicative type, then \(D(Q)\) is a « finitely generated twisted constant group » (SGA 3 X 5.1), as was seen in (SGA 3 IX 4.5 and X 5.7). If \(P\) is a group scheme, every extension of \(P\) by \(D(Q)\) is thus representable, by a known lemma of fpqc descent theory (SGA 3 X 5.4).

3.3.4 It is false that for every group scheme \(Q\) affine, flat and of finite presentation over \(S\), we have \(\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^1(Q,G_m)=0\), already for \(Q=G_{aS}\), \(S=\operatorname{Spec} k[t]/(t^2)\), \(k\) being a field of char. \(p>0\).

Proposition 3.4.

Let \(P\) be a smooth group scheme of finite presentation over \(S\) with connected fibers, \(Q\) a torus over \(S\). Then the category of biextensions of \((P,Q)\) by \(G_m\) is equivalent to the punctual category, i.e. we have

\[(3.4.1)\qquad \operatorname{Biext}^0(P,Q;G_m)=\operatorname{Biext}^1(P,Q;G_m)=0.\]

Indeed, let \(M=D(Q)\), which is a twisted constant group over \(S\) with fibers isomorphic to copies of \(\mathbb Z^r\) (3.3.3). By virtue of 3.3.2, the category under consideration is equivalent to that of the extensions of \(P\) by \(M\). This category is rigid, i.e. the group of automorphisms of an object is reduced to the unit group, which expresses the first relation (3.4.1), which can also be written \(\operatorname{Hom}(P,M)=0\). Now this relation follows at once from the fact that \(M\) is étale over \(S\) and \(P\) has connected fibers. It thus remains to prove the relation
\[\operatorname{Ext}^1(P,M)=0,\]

i.e. that every extension \(E\) of \(P\) by \(M\) is trivial. Using the uniqueness of a splitting of this extension (if one exists), we are reduced to proving that \(E\) is locally trivial, which reduces us to the case where \(M=\mathbb Z_S^r\), and even to the case where \(M=\mathbb Z_S\). When \(S\) is the spectrum of a field, the conclusion follows from 5.5 (i) below. (NB we shall not use 3.4 before IX 6, and in particular there will be no vicious circle!). Let us now show how to reduce the general case to this particular case. Let us note that it suffices to find a section \(f\) of \(E\) over \(P\) such that \(f(0)=0\), for such a section will necessarily be multiplicative, i.e. will satisfy \(f(x+y)-f(x)-f(y)=0\): indeed, the first member of this relation denotes a morphism \(P\times_S P\longrightarrow\mathbb Z_S\) which is null on the null section of \(P\times_S P\), hence is null since \(P\times_S P\) has connected fibers (\(P\) having geometrically connected fibers). The desired conclusion then follows from the

Lemma 3.4.2.

Let \(S\) be a scheme, \(P\) a smooth \(S\)-scheme of finite presentation, with geometrically connected fibers, \(Z\) a group, \(E\) a \(Z_P\)-torsor (for the fppf topology, the étale topology or the fpqc topology, it comes to the same, cf. SGA 3 X 5.4 ...), \(e\) a section of \(P\) over \(S\), \(e'\) a section of \(E\) over \(S\) above \(e\) (i.e. a section of \(e^{-1}(E)\) over \(S\)). For \(E\) to be trivial, it is (necessary and) sufficient that for every \(s\in S\), the induced torsor \(E_s\) of \(P_s\) be so, and then there exists a unique section \(f\) of \(E\) over \(P\) such that \(f\circ e=e'\).

The uniqueness of such a section is clear, for two such sections \(f,g\) « differ » by a morphism \(h:P\longrightarrow Z_S\) such that \(h\circ e=1\), and such a morphism is constant with value 1 since \(P\) has connected fibers. Moreover, if there exists a section \(f\) of \(E\) over \(P\), one can always correct it by a morphism \(P\longrightarrow Z_S\) (coming from a morphism \(z:S\longrightarrow Z_S\)) so as to obtain a section \(f'\) such that \(f'\circ e=e'\): it suffices to choose \(z\) such that \(f(e(t))=e'(t)z(t)\). Applying this last observation to the fibers above the \(s\in S\), one finds for every point \(s\in S\) a well-determined section \(f_s\) of \(E_s\) over \(P_s\), transforming \(e_s\) into \(e'_s\). Everything comes down to proving that these \(f_s\) come from a section \(f\) of \(E\) over \(P\), or what amounts to the same (EGA IV 17.9.3), that the set \(U\subset E\) union of the \(f_s(P_s)\) is an open subset of \(E\). Suppose first \(S\) noetherian; then one verifies easily that \(U\) is constructible, using the fact that for every \(s\in S\), there exists an open neighborhood \(T'\) of \(s\) in \(T=\) the closure of \(s\) equipped with the induced reduced structure, and a section of \(E_{T'}\) over \(P_{T'}\) extending \(f_s\) and compatible with the marked sections \(e\) and \(e'\) (as follows trivially from EGA IV 8.8.2). By virtue of the criterion EGA IV 1.10.1, it then remains to prove that \(U\) is stable under generization. For this, we are reduced, by the usual method using EGA II 7.1.9, to the case where \(S\) is the spectrum of a discrete valuation ring. As \(P\) is smooth over \(S\), \(P\) is thus regular hence normal, and as it has connected fibers, it is connected. But then the given section of \(E\) above the generic fiber of \(P\) over \(S\) extends in a unique way to a section \(f\) of \(E\) over \(P\) (EGA IV 18.10.19 and 18.10.20), evidently compatible with \(e\) and \(e'\). Hence in this case \(U=f(P)\) is open. This proves 3.4 in the case where \(S\) is noetherian. The general case reduces easily to this case, by the usual limit-passage procedures, using EGA IV 8,9, whose details we leave to the reader.
Remark 3.4.3. One can « crush » the lemma 3.4.2 by learned references, namely (SGA 4 XV 1.15, case n=1, and 4.1).

Corollary 3.5.

Let \(P\) be a smooth group scheme of finite presentation over \(S\), with connected fibers, \(0\longrightarrow Q'\longrightarrow Q\longrightarrow Q''\longrightarrow0\) an exact sequence of commutative Groups over \(S\), with \(Q'\) a torus. Then the functor « inverse image of biextensions »

\[(3.5.1)\qquad \operatorname{BIEXT}(P,Q'';G_m)\xrightarrow{\sim} \operatorname{BIEXT}(P,Q;G_m)\]

is an equivalence of categories, in particular the following natural homomorphisms are isomorphisms

\[(3.5.2)\qquad \operatorname{Biext}^i(P,Q'';G_m)\xrightarrow{\sim} \operatorname{Biext}^i(P,Q;G_m),\qquad i=0,1.\]

Indeed, \(\operatorname{BIEXT}(P,Q'';G_m)\) will be identified, up to equivalence, with the category of biextensions \(E\) of \((P,Q)\) by \(G_m\) equipped with a trivialization of the induced biextension \(E'\) of \((P,Q')\) by \(G_m\) (VII 3.7.6). Now for a given biextension, there is by virtue of 3.4 exactly one trivialization of \(E'\). Of course, one can equally prove the fact

that \(\operatorname{Biext}^i(P,Q'';G_m)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Biext}^i(P,Q;G_m)\) is an isomorphism for \(i=0,1\) (which is equivalent to the conclusion of 3.5) by using the exact sequence (VII 3.7.5) of the \(\operatorname{Biext}^i\) associated with the exact sequence \(0\longrightarrow Q'\longrightarrow Q\longrightarrow Q''\longrightarrow0\).

Remark 3.6. Of course, the symmetric statement deduced from 3.5, obtained by exchanging left and right, is equally valid, and is moreover deduced formally from 3.5 by the operation of passage to symmetric biextensions (VII 2.7). When one assumes, in addition to the data and hypotheses of 3.5, that one has the symmetric data and hypotheses, i.e. \(Q\) has connected fibers (or again \(Q''\) with connected fibers, which comes to the same since \(Q'\) has connected fibers), and that we have an exact sequence \(0\longrightarrow P'\longrightarrow P\longrightarrow P''\longrightarrow0\) with \(P'\) a torus, then the successive application of 3.5, and of the symmetric statement applied to the preceding exact sequence and to \(Q''\), show that the functor
\[(3.6.1)\qquad \operatorname{BIEXT}(P'',Q'';G_m)\xrightarrow{\sim} \operatorname{BIEXT}(P,Q;G_m)\]
is an equivalence of categories, and in particular the following homomorphism is an isomorphism
\[(3.6.2)\qquad \operatorname{Biext}^i(P'',Q'';G_m)\xrightarrow{\sim} \operatorname{Biext}^i(P,Q;G_m),\qquad i=0,1.\]

Proposition 3.7.

Let \(A\) be an abelian scheme over \(S\), \(T\) a group scheme of multiplicative type (SGA 3 IX) and of finite type over \(S\), \(M=\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(T,G_m)\) the Cartier dual of \(T\) (which is a finitely generated twisted constant group over \(S\) (3.3.3)). Then the categories \(\operatorname{EXT}(A,T)\) and \(\operatorname{BIEXT}(A,M;G_m)\), formed respectively of the commutative extensions of \(A\) by \(T\) and of the biextensions of \((A,M)\) by \(G_m\), are rigid, and canonically equivalent, and the groups of classes of objects of these categories are canonically isomorphic to \(\operatorname{Hom}(M,A^*)\), where \(A^*=\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^1(A,G_m)\) is the dual abelian scheme of \(A\) (3.2). In particular, we have a canonical isomorphism

\[(3.7.1)\qquad \operatorname{Ext}^1(A,T)\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Hom}(M,A^*).\]

This is in fact the particular case of 1.6 obtained by taking \(P=A\), \(Q=M\); by virtue of the biduality theorem (SGA 3 X 5.7), we indeed have \(T\xrightarrow{\sim}\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(M,G_m)\), and it suffices to verify the conditions (1.6.1), which here read
\[\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(A,G_m)=0,\qquad \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(A,\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^1(M,G_m))=0.\]
The first relation has already been recalled in (3.2.1); the second can be sharpened to
\[\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^1(M,G_m)=0.\]

To verify the latter, the question being local on \(S\), we may assume \(M\) constant \(=M_S\), and by decomposing \(M\) into a sum of cyclic \(\mathbb Z\)-modules, we are reduced to the case where \(M=\mathbb Z\), which is trivial, and to the case \(M=\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z\), which comes from the fact that \(G_m\) is \(n\)-divisible (or, more learnedly, from 3.3.1).

One will note that when \(T=G_m\) hence \(M=\mathbb Z_S\), (3.7.1) reduces to the identity isomorphism (3.2.3). Of course, the direct proof of (3.7.1) would be essentially trivial (without bringing in the group \(\operatorname{Ext}^1(A\mathbin{\overset{\mathbf L}{\otimes}}M,G_m)=\operatorname{Biext}^1(A,M;G_m)\)).

pages.title: 4. Extensions and biextensions of smooth connected group schemes over a field

blocks.html:
4. Extensions and biextensions of smooth connected group schemes
over a field
Let us recall the following lemma due to Rosenlicht [3 bis, Prop.3]:

Lemma 4.1.

Let \(k\) be a field, \(X\) and \(Y\) two \(k\)-schemes of finite type, separable and geometrically connected, equipped with points \(e_X\) resp. \(e_Y\) rational over \(k\). Then every morphism \(X\times Y\longrightarrow G_m\) which is trivial on the two subschemes \(X\times e_Y\) and \(e_X\times Y\) is trivial.

We shall use this result only when \(X\) and \(Y\) are group schemes over \(k\), in which case it will follow from the hypotheses made that \(X\) and \(Y\) are even smooth over \(k\). Let us note that by induction, the result 4.1 implies the analogous result for an arbitrary number of factors. This therefore allows us to apply the results of VII 1.3 and of VII 2.9. One thus obtains the two following propositions:

Proposition 4.2.

Let \(P\) be a smooth and connected group over the field \(k\). Then the natural functor from the category of extensions of Groups of \(P\) by \(G_m\) into the category of invertible Modules on \(P\) rigidified at the neutral element \(e_P\) of \(P\) is fully faithful. Its essential image is formed of the rigidified invertible Modules \(L\) on \(P\) such that we have \(\pi^*(L)\simeq\operatorname{pr}_1^*(L)\operatorname{pr}_2^*(L)\), where \(\pi:P\times P\longrightarrow P\) is the composition law of \(P\), and \(\operatorname{pr}_1\) and \(\operatorname{pr}_2\) are the two projections of the product.

Proposition 4.3.

Let \(P\) and \(Q\) be two smooth and connected algebraic Groups over the field \(k\). Then the natural functor from the category of biextensions of \((P,Q)\) by \(G_m\), into the category of invertible Modules on \(P\times Q\) birigidified with respect to the unit sections \(e_P,e_Q\) of \(P\) resp. \(Q\), is fully faithful, the two categories under consideration being moreover rigid. For a birigidified invertible Module \(L\) on \(P\times Q\) to come from a biextension, it is necessary and sufficient that its inverse images on \(P\times P\times Q\) and \(P\times Q\times Q\) by \(\pi_P\times\operatorname{id}_Q\) and \(\operatorname{id}_P\times\pi_Q\) satisfy the « theorem of the cube » ([1] or [2]).
This last condition is in fact only a translation of the conditions considered in VII 2.9.3 b), in the explicitation of them given in VII 3.5 b).

4.4. I do not know whether the cube condition considered in 4.3 is automatically satisfied. One knows at least [2, IV 2.6] that for a given \(L\), there always exists an integer \(n\geq1\), a power of the characteristic exponent of \(k\), such that \(L^{\otimes n}\) satisfies the condition under consideration, hence comes from a biextension of \((P,Q)\) by \(G_m\); and that one can always find a radicial extension \(k'\) of \(k\), such that the desired condition becomes true after the base extension \(k\longrightarrow k'\), so that after this extension \(L\) gives a birigidified invertible Module which comes from a biextension of \((P,Q)\) by \(G_m\). In particular, if \(k\) is perfect, the condition considered in 4.3 is always satisfied. This last fact, which formally implies the preceding ones by a trace argument, can also be seen by applying 4.7 further below, cf. 4.9.
Let us recall in the form of a lemma the following result of EGA IV Err., 53, 21.4.13:

Lemma 4.5.

Let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) be a smooth surjective morphism with integral fibers, with \(Y\) normal integral with generic point \(y\), \(L\) an invertible Module on \(X\) whose restriction to \(X_y\) is trivial. Then there exists an invertible Module \(M\) on \(Y\) such that \(L\) is isomorphic to \(f^*(M)\).

Proposition 4.6.

Let \(P,Q\) be two smooth and connected algebraic groups over the field \(k\), with \(P\) affine. One assumes moreover that \(P\) admits a composition series each of whose factors is a torus, or is isomorphic to the additive group \(G_a\) (a condition satisfied in particular if \(k\) is perfect [SGA 3, XVII 7.2.1, 4.1.1 (i) \(\Longleftrightarrow\) (ii) and 4.1.5]), or that \(Q\) is an extension of an abelian variety \(B\) by a smooth, connected and affine algebraic group satisfying the preceding condition. Under these conditions, every birigidified invertible Module \(L\) on \(P\times Q\) is trivial, a fortiori (4.3) every biextension of \((P,Q)\) by \(G_m\) is trivial.

Let us first point out that as every automorphism of the birigidified Module is the identity (4.3), descent theory shows us that it suffices to prove that \(L\) becomes trivial after a finite separable extension of \(k\), which reduces us at once, by passage to the limit, to the case where \(k\) is separably closed. As in this case every torus over \(k\) is split, one sees that if \(P\) admits a composition series as indicated in 4.6, it also admits a composition series with quotients isomorphic to \(G_a\) or \(G_m\), which implies, by repeated application of 4.5, taking into account \(\operatorname{Pic}(G_{aK})=\operatorname{Pic}(G_{mK})=0\) for every field \(K\), that we have
\[\operatorname{Pic}(P)=0.\]

As this relation will still remain true under any change of the base field \(k\), one sees, by applying 4.5 to \(\operatorname{pr}_2:P\times Q\longrightarrow Q\) and to the Module \(L\), that \(L\) is isomorphic to a Module of the form \(\operatorname{pr}_2^*(M)\), where \(M\) is an invertible Module on \(Q\). As the restriction of \(L\) to \(e_P\times Q\) is trivial, one concludes at once that \(M\) is trivial, hence \(L\) is trivial, which establishes the conclusion in the first case considered. In the second, where \(Q\) is an extension of an abelian scheme \(B\) by a group \(M\) admitting a composition series with quotients isomorphic to \(G_m\) or to \(G_a\), one applies 4.5 to the morphism
\[f:X=P\times Q\longrightarrow Y=P\times B,\]

whose fiber at the generic point \(y\) of \(P\times B\) is a torsor under \(M_y\), necessarily trivial by virtue of the particular structure of \(M\) and the classical relations \(H^1(K,G_a)=0\), \(H^1(K,G_m)=0\), valid for every field \(K\) (which we shall here take equal to \(k(y)\)). As we shall have \(\operatorname{Pic}(M)=0\), hence \(\operatorname{Pic}(X_y)=0\), 4.5 is indeed applicable and shows that \(L\) is isomorphic to the inverse image of an invertible Module \(M\) on \(Y=P\times B\). After replacing \(M\) by \(M\operatorname{pr}_1^*(M_1)^{-1}\operatorname{pr}_2^*(M_2)^{-1}\), where \(\operatorname{pr}_1\) and \(\operatorname{pr}_2\) are the two projections of \(Y=P\times B\), and where \(M_1\) and \(M_2\) are the two restrictions of \(M\) to \(P\times e_B\) and to \(e_P\times B\) respectively, we may assume \(M\) birigidified, and that \(L\) is its birigidified inverse image. This reduces us to the case where \(Q\) is itself an abelian scheme. But then \(L\) defines a canonical morphism
\[(*)\qquad P\longrightarrow \operatorname{Pic}_{Q/k}^0,\]

transforming unit into unit, and as \(P\) is connected, this morphism factors through the dual abelian scheme \(Q'=\operatorname{Pic}_{Q/k}^0\). Now it is well known that a morphism of schemes from a smooth and connected affine algebraic group into an abelian scheme is constant. (It is a « geometric » question i.e. one may assume \(k\) algebraically closed, and in this case one is reduced at once by dévissage to the case where \(P\) is \(G_a\) or \(G_m\); now it is well known that every rational map from \(\mathbb P_k^1\) into an abelian scheme over \(k\) is constant.) The fact that (*) is constant, hence null, is interpreted by the condition that \(L\) be isomorphic to the inverse image of an invertible Module \(M\) on \(P\), and as \(L\) is trivial on \(P\times e_A\), \(M\) is trivial, hence \(L\) is also trivial, qed.

Proposition 4.7.

Let \(P,Q\) be two smooth and connected algebraic groups over the field \(k\). Suppose that \(P\) is an extension of an abelian scheme \(A\) by a smooth, connected and affine algebraic group \(L\) admitting a composition series each of whose factors is isomorphic to a torus or to \(G_a\) (a condition always fulfilled if \(k\) is perfect). Then the functor considered in 1.3 is an equivalence of categories, i.e. every birigidified invertible Module \(L\) on \(P\times Q\) comes from a biextension of \((P,Q)\). Moreover, the functor of rigid categories

\[\operatorname{BIEXT}(A,Q;G_m)\longrightarrow\operatorname{BIEXT}(P,Q;G_m)\]

is an equivalence of categories, in other words, it gives rise to an isomorphism of groups:

\[(4.7.1)\qquad \operatorname{Biext}^1(A,Q;G_m)\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Biext}^1(P,Q;G_m).\]

For the first assertion, one reduces again, by descent and using 4.3, to the case where \(k\) is separably closed. Proceeding as in the proof of 4.6, one deduces from 4.5 and from the hypothesis on \(L\) that \(L\) is isomorphic to the inverse image of a birigidified Module \(M\) on \(A\times Q\), and for the first assertion of 4.7, we are thus reduced to the case \(A=P\). Now \(L\) defines a morphism of pointed schemes
\[f:Q\longrightarrow\operatorname{Pic}_{P/k},\]
which necessarily factors through the dual abelian scheme \(P'\) of \(P\). Let \(L'\) be the inverse image of the Weil sheaf on \(P\times P'\) by
\[\operatorname{id}_P\times f:P\times Q\longrightarrow P\times P',\]

it is a birigidified Module which, by definition of \(f\), differs from \(L\) only modulo the inverse image of an invertible Module \(M\) on \(P\). As \(L'\) and \(L\) have trivial restriction to \(P\times e_Q\), one concludes that \(M\) is trivial, hence that \(L\) is isomorphic to \(L'\). Now we pointed out in VII 2.9.4 that on a product of two abelian schemes, divisorial correspondences come from biextensions. It follows that \(L'\), or what amounts to the same, \(L\), comes from a biextension.

Returning to the general case (\(P\) an extension of \(A\) by \(L\)), it remains to prove that (4.7.1) is indeed an isomorphism. But this follows from the exact sequence of the \(\operatorname{Biext}^i\) (\(i=0,1\)) associated with the exact sequence
\[0\longrightarrow L\longrightarrow P\longrightarrow A\longrightarrow0,\]
and from the relations
\[\operatorname{Biext}^0(L,Q;G_m)=0,\qquad \operatorname{Biext}^1(L,Q;G_m)=0,\]
the first of which is a particular case of the rigidity assertion made in 4.3, and the second is none other than the first case of 4.6.

Corollary 4.8.

Under the conditions of 4.7, suppose that \(Q\) is an extension of an algebraic group \(B\) (necessarily smooth and connected) by an algebraic group \(M\) which is smooth, connected and affine. Then the functor of rigid categories

\[\operatorname{BIEXT}(A,B;G_m)\longrightarrow\operatorname{BIEXT}(P,Q;G_m)\]

is an equivalence of categories, in other words, it gives rise to an isomorphism of groups

\[(4.8.1)\qquad \operatorname{Biext}^1(A,B;G_m)\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Biext}^1(P,Q;G_m).\]
Taking (4.7.1) into account, it remains to prove that we have an isomorphism
\[\operatorname{Biext}^1(A,B;G_m)\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Biext}^1(A,Q;G_m).\]
This is deduced like (4.7.1) from the exact sequence of the \(\operatorname{Biext}^i\) deduced from the exact sequence
\[0\longrightarrow M\longrightarrow Q\longrightarrow B\longrightarrow0,\]
and from the relations
\[\operatorname{Biext}^0(P,M;G_m)=0,\qquad \operatorname{Biext}^1(P,M;G_m)=0,\]
the second of which is a particular case of the second case considered in 4.6 (where the role of the two factors considered would have been exchanged).

Remark 4.9. Let \(P,Q\) be two smooth and connected algebraic groups over a perfect field \(k\). One knows then [3] that each of them is an extension of an abelian scheme by a smooth, connected and affine group, the latter being the product of a torus by a multiple extension of groups \(G_a\) (cf. reference given in 4.6):
\[0\longrightarrow L\longrightarrow P\longrightarrow A\longrightarrow0,\qquad 0\longrightarrow M\longrightarrow Q\longrightarrow B\longrightarrow0.\]
Hence 4.8 applies, and shows us that the theory of biextensions of \((P,Q)\) by \(G_m\) reduces to the analogous theory (and how classical a one!) for the pair \((A,B)\) of abelian varieties.

Corollary 4.10.

Let \(P\) and \(Q\) be two smooth and connected algebraic groups over \(k\), \(L\) a smooth and connected affine algebraic subgroup of \(P\), \(E\) a biextension of \((P,Q)\) by \(G_m\), whence by 2.2 a pairing deduced from \(E\)

\[T_\ell(P)\times T_\ell(Q)\longrightarrow T_\ell(G_m).\]

Then the induced pairing

\[T_\ell(L)\times T_\ell(Q)\longrightarrow T_\ell(G_m)\]

is null.

Indeed, we may evidently assume \(k\) algebraically closed, in which case we are under the conditions of 4.6, which imply that the restriction of \(E\) to \((L,Q)\) is the trivial biextension, whence the conclusion, since the induced pairing under consideration is the one defined by this restriction.

4.11. Let \(P,Q\) be two smooth algebraic groups over \(k\), \(E\) a biextension of \((P,Q)\) by \(G_m\), and \(T\) the largest subtorus of \(P\). We propose to determine the left annihilator of the pairing
\[(4.11.1)\qquad T_\ell(P)\times T_\ell(Q)\longrightarrow T_\ell(G_{m_k}),\]

\(\ell\) being again a fixed prime number. By virtue of 4.10, this annihilator contains in any case the sub-pro-group \(T_\ell(T)\) of \(T_\ell(P)\). Let us consider an extension \(k'\) of \(k\) over which \(P\) and \(Q\) can be written as an extension of an abelian scheme by an affine group scheme, a multiple extension of tori and of groups \(G_a\); when \(P\) and \(Q\) have null unipotent ranks, one can take \(k'=k\); in any case, one can take for \(k'\) the perfect closure (or a finite radicial extension) of \(k\). Let \(A'\) resp. \(B'\) be the abelian part of \(P_{k'}\) resp. \(Q_{k'}\). By virtue of 4.7, the biextension \(E_{k'}\) of \((P_{k'},Q_{k'})\) comes from a biextension \(F'\) of \((A',B')\), well determined up to unique isomorphism. Hence the pairing
\[(4.11.2)\qquad T_\ell(P_{k'})\times T_\ell(Q_{k'})\longrightarrow T_\ell(G_{m_{k'}})\]

deduced from (4.11.1) by change of the base field \(k\longrightarrow k'\), and which is also the pairing defined by the biextension \(E_{k'}\) of \((P_{k'},Q_{k'})\), is obtained by means of the pairing
\[(4.11.3)\qquad T_\ell(A')\times T_\ell(B')\longrightarrow T_\ell(G_{m_{k'}})\]
defined by the biextension \(F'\), by composing with the canonical homomorphisms (which are in fact epimorphisms if \(P\) and \(Q\) are \(\ell\)-divisible).
\[(4.11.4)\qquad T_\ell(P_{k'})\longrightarrow T_\ell(A'),\quad T_\ell(Q_{k'})\longrightarrow T_\ell(B').\]

Consequently, the left annihilator of the pairing (4.11.2) (which is deduced from the left annihilator of (4.11.1) by base change) is none other than the inverse image by the first homomorphism (4.11.4) of the left annihilator of the pairing (4.11.3). If \(C'\) is the largest abelian subscheme of \(A'\) such that the restriction of \(F'\) to \((C',B')\) is the trivial biextension (hence \(C'\) is also the reduced neutral component of the homomorphism
\[(4.11.5)\qquad A'\longrightarrow \text{dual abelian scheme of }B'\]

defined by \(F'\)), the left annihilator of (4.11.3) is identified with \(T_\ell(C')\); hence the left annihilator of the pairing (1.11.1) is none other than the inverse image of \(T_\ell(C')\longrightarrow T_\ell(A')\) by the first morphism (4.11.4).

In particular, to say that the left annihilator of the pairing (4.11.1) is reduced to \(T_\ell(T)\), i.e. that the left annihilator of (4.11.2) is reduced to the kernel \(T_\ell(T')\) of the first morphism (4.11.4), amounts to saying that the pairing (4.11.3) is left separating i.e. that \(C'=0\), or again that the morphism (4.11.5) defined by \(F'\) has finite kernel. One will note under this last form that this condition does not depend on \(\ell\), and it is clear (under any form whatsoever) that it does not depend on the choice of the extension \(k'\). When this condition is fulfilled, we shall say simply that the given biextension \(E\) of \((P,Q)\) by \(G_m\) is left separating. One will note by duality that this also means that the morphism
\[(4.11.6)\qquad B'\longrightarrow \text{dual abelian scheme of }A'\]
defined by \(F'\) is an epimorphism (i.e. surjective).

One defines symmetrically the notion of a biextension separating on the right, which corresponds to the case where (4.11.6) has finite kernel, i.e. (4.11.5) is an epimorphism. One says that the biextension \(E\) of \((P,Q)\) by \(G_{m_k}\) is separating if it is separating on the left and on the right, which amounts to saying that (4.11.5) is an isogeny, or what comes to the same, that (4.11.6) is an isogeny.
It is evident by definition that these notions are invariant under any extension of the base field \(k\).
4.12. Let \(P\) be a smooth and connected group scheme, \(L\) an invertible Module on \(P\), and let us set
\[(4.12.1)\qquad \delta(L)=\pi^*(L)\,\operatorname{pr}_1^*(L)^{-1}\operatorname{pr}_2^*(L)^{-1},\]

the notations being those of 4.2. As we have already pointed out in VII 1.3.4, we thus find a birigidified invertible Module on \(P\times P\). By virtue of 4.3, if \(\delta(L)\) comes from a biextension, the latter is determined up to unique isomorphism. This biextension is defined in particular, by virtue of 4.7, if \(P\) is an extension of an abelian scheme \(A\) by a multiple extension of tori and of groups \(G_a\). In any case, if it is defined, it is clear that this biextension is symmetric. In the preceding favorable case, and when the maximal torus of \(P\) is assumed to be split, we can reduce the construction of \(\delta(L)\) to the case where \(P\) is an abelian scheme, by noting that it is evidently functorial in \(P\), and that on the other hand the homomorphism
\[(4.12.2)\qquad \operatorname{Pic}(A)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Pic}(P)\]
deduced from the canonical homomorphism \(f:A\longrightarrow P\) is surjective thanks to 4.5 (cf. beginning of the proof of 4.6), so that we can find an isomorphism
\[L\simeq f^*(M),\]
where \(M\) is an invertible Module on \(A\). Then \(\delta(L)\) "is" the inverse image of the biextension \(\delta(M)\) of \((A,A)\) by \(G_{m_k}\).

Proposition 4.13.

Under the conditions of 4.12, suppose that \(L\) is an ample invertible Module on \(P\). Then if the biextension \(\delta(L)\) of \((P,P)\) by \(G_{m_k}\) is defined, it is separating (4.11); if \(P\) is an extension of an abelian scheme \(A\) by a smooth connected affine group \(L\) satisfying the condition of 4.7, then the biextension of \((A,A)\) from which \(\delta(L)\) comes (by virtue of 4.8) is of the form \(\delta(M)\), where \(M\) is an ample invertible Module on \(A\).

We are reduced to the case where \(k\) is algebraically closed. By definition, it then suffices to prove, with the notations of 4.12, that the biextension \(\delta(M)\) of \((A,A)\) is separating. Now it is proved in the thesis of M. RAYNAUD [2, XI 1.11] that if \(L=f^*(M)\) is ample, the same is true of \(M\). Now it is well known from the theory of A. WEIL that this implies that \(\delta(M)\) is separating [1] [2], i.e. that the homomorphism from \(A\) into its dual abelian scheme is an isogeny.

Remark 4.14. Let us place ourselves in the favorable case where \(P\) is an extension of an abelian scheme \(A\) by a group \(L\) which is a multiple extension of tori and of groups \(G_a\), and consider the canonical composite homomorphism
\[(4.14.1)\qquad \operatorname{NS}(A)\xrightarrow{\delta} \operatorname{Biext}^1(A,A;G_m)\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Biext}^1(P,P;G_m).\]

We know by 4.8 that the second arrow is bijective; on the other hand it is well known in the theory of abelian varieties that the first arrow induces an isomorphism from the Néron–Severi group \(\operatorname{NS}(A)\) of \(A\) onto the subgroup of \(\operatorname{Biext}^1(A,A;G_m)=\operatorname{Corr}(A,A)\) formed by the (classes of) biextensions (\(\simeq\) divisorial correspondences) which are symmetric. From this it follows that the composite (4.14.1) induces an isomorphism of \(\operatorname{NS}(A)\) with the group of symmetric biextensions of \((P,P)\) by \(G_m\) (which, by virtue of 4.7, can moreover also be described as the group of (classes of) divisorial correspondences on \((P,P)\) which are symmetric. It is natural to call a divisorial correspondence on \((P,P)\) an ample divisorial correspondence, and to call again ample biextension of \((P,P)\) by \(G_m\) the corresponding biextension, when it comes from an ample element of the group \(\operatorname{NS}(A)\). This condition being manifestly invariant under change of the base field, we see that the preceding notion keeps its meaning without the restrictive condition placed above on \(P\), assuming only that \(P\) is smooth and connected.

Note that, when we suppose even that \(L\) is "solvable over \(k\)", i.e. that it admits a dévissage into groups \(G_m\) and \(G_a\), it follows from the preceding observation, and from the fact that the map (4.12.2) is surjective, that the image of
\[(4.14.2)\qquad \operatorname{Pic}(P)\xrightarrow{\delta}\operatorname{Biext}^1(P,P;G_m)\]

is formed by the symmetric biextensions, and that its kernel is the subgroup \(\operatorname{Pic}^0(P)\) of \(\operatorname{Pic}(P)\) which is the image of \(\operatorname{Pic}^0(A)\), so that, setting
\[\operatorname{NS}(P)=\operatorname{Pic}(P)/\operatorname{Pic}^0(P)\quad(\simeq\operatorname{NS}(A)),\]
we can also interpret the composite (4.14.1) as a canonical isomorphism
\[(4.14.3)\qquad \operatorname{NS}(P)\xrightarrow{\sim} \operatorname{Biext}^1(P,P;G_m)^{\mathrm{sym}}.\]

Let us note finally that the theorem of RAYNAUD cited for 4.13 can be stated by saying that an element \(\xi\) of \(\operatorname{Pic}(P)\) is ample if and only if its image \(\delta(\xi)\) under 4.14.1 is a class of ample biextensions.

pages.title: 5. Extensions and biextensions by torsion-free truncated constant group schemes

blocks.html: 5. Extensions and biextensions by torsion-free truncated constant group schemes
We give, in this section and the following one, some auxiliary results, which will be used in section 7 in the case \(Z=\mathbb Z\).

Proposition 5.1.

Let \(S\) be a geometrically unibranch and irreducible scheme with generic point \(\eta\), \(Z\) a torsion-free group, whence a constant group scheme \(Z_S\) over \(S\). Then every torsor \(E\) under the group scheme \(Z_S\) is trivial, and the functor \(E\longmapsto E(\eta)\) from the category of torsors under \(Z_S\) to the category of torsors under \(Z=Z_S(\eta)\) is an equivalence of categories.

A well-known lemma of descent theory (SGA 3 X 5.4) shows us that every torsor \(E\) under \(G_S\) is representable (this result being valid for every group scheme over \(S\) which is separated, locally of finite presentation and locally quasi-finite over \(S\)). Since \(Z_S\) is étale over \(S\), the same is true by fpqc descent of every torsor \(E\) under \(Z_S\), which is therefore locally trivial for the étale topology and not only the fppf topology). To prove that \(E\) is trivial, it thus suffices to prove that we have
and similarly the full faithfulness assertion of 5.1 reduces to the relation
\[H^0(S_{\mathrm{ét}},Z_S)\xleftarrow{\sim} Z.\]
The latter being trivial, let us prove the first. For this, consider the inclusion
\[i:\eta\longrightarrow S,\]
we then have, thanks to the fact that \(S\) is geometrically unibranch
\[i_*(Z_\eta)\xleftarrow{\sim}Z_S\]
(SGA 4 IX 2.14.1), whence by the non-commutative exact sequence of low degrees standing in for the Leray spectral sequence for \(i:\eta\longrightarrow S\) (SGA 4 XII 3.2):
\[e\longrightarrow H^1(S,i_*(Z_\eta))\longrightarrow H^1(\eta,Z_\eta)\longrightarrow\cdots,\]

the fact that \(H^1(S,Z_S)\longrightarrow H^1(\eta,Z_\eta)\) is injective. We are thus reduced to the case where \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\). If \(\pi\) is the Galois group of a separable closure of \(k\), we will then have
\[H^1(k,Z)=\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{cont}}(\pi,Z)/Z=e,\]
since \(E\) is torsion-free. This completes the proof.

Corollary 5.2.

With the notations of 5.1, let \(P\) be a smooth scheme over \(S\). Then every torsor \(E\) under \(Z_P\) is trivial, and the functor \(E\longmapsto E_\eta\) from the category of torsors over \(P\), with group \(Z_P\), to the category of torsors over \(P_\eta\) with group \(Z_{P_\eta}\), is an equivalence of categories.

Indeed, \(P\) is geometrically unibranch (EGA IV 17.5.7), and since each irreducible component of \(P\) dominates \(S\), \(P\) being flat over \(S\), the set of irreducible components of \(P\) is locally finite. Hence \(P\) is a prescheme which is a sum of geometrically unibranch irreducible preschemes \(P_i\). This said, 5.2 follows at once from 5.1 applied to the various \(P_i\) and to the \(P_{i\eta}\), taking into account that the generic point \(p_i\) of \(P_i\) is also a generic point of \(P_{i\eta}\).

5.2.1. From the preceding corollary there follows at once the analogous statement for the bitorsors over \(P\) under \((Z_P,Z_P)\): the functor \(E\longmapsto E_\eta\) from the category of these, to the category of bitorsors over \(P_\eta\) under \((Z_{P_\eta},Z_{P_\eta})\), is an equivalence of categories. Indeed, it suffices to make explicit the structure of bitorsor on \(E\) in terms of that of right torsor under \(Z_P\), equipped with an isomorphism from \(Z_P\) to the commutant of \(Z_P\) for its operation on \(E\), using the fact that \(E\) is necessarily trivial and hence that the commutant in question is isomorphic to \(Z_P\) itself. We are thus reduced to the following trivial remark for the irreducible components \(X\) of \(P\): if \(X\) is a non-empty connected scheme, the functor \(Z\longmapsto Z_X\) from the category of sets to that of \(X\)-schemes is fully faithful.

Proposition 5.3.

Let \(S\) and \(Z\) be as in 5.1, and \(P\) a group scheme smooth over \(S\). Consider the extensions \(E\) of group schemes of \(P\) by \(Z_S\). Then the functor \(E\longmapsto E_\eta\), going from the category of these extensions to that of extensions of group schemes of \(P_\eta\) by \(Z_\eta\), is an equivalence of categories. If \(P\) and \(Z\) are commutative, this functor also induces an equivalence between the corresponding categories of commutative extensions.

Let us note first that every extension of sheaves of groups of \(P\) by \(Z_S\) (or of \(P_\eta\) by \(Z_\eta\)) gives rise by 5.2 to a trivial torsor, and a fortiori a representable one. Hence the categories considered in 5.3 can also be interpreted as categories of extensions of sheaves of groups. The latter are amenable to the description given in VII 1.1.6 and 1.2 in terms of bitorsors over \(P\), \(P\times P\), \(P\times P\times P\) (resp. over \(P_\eta\), \(P_\eta\times P_\eta\), \(P_\eta\times P_\eta\times P_\eta\)), under the pairs of groups deduced from \((Z,Z)\) by base change. Applying 5.2 in the case of commutative extensions, and 5.2.1 in the general case, to the schemes (smooth over \(S\)) \(P\), \(P\times P\) and \(P\times P\times P\), we obtain the announced conclusion. In an entirely analogous way one finds:

Corollary 5.4.

Let \(S,Z\) be as in 5.1, and \(P,Q\) two group schemes smooth over \(S\). We suppose \(P,Q,Z\) commutative, and we consider the biextensions \(E\) of \((P,Q)\) by \(Z_S\). Then the functor \(E\longmapsto E_\eta\) which goes from the category of these biextensions to the category of biextensions of \((P_\eta,Q_\eta)\) by \(Z_\eta\), is an equivalence of categories.

The preceding results thus lead us to determine the categories of extensions or of biextensions by a torsion-free constant group, in the case of a base scheme reduced to the spectrum of a field. One then finds the following:

Proposition 5.5.

Let \(k\) be a field, \(Z\) a torsion-free group, \(P\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite type, \(P^0\) the identity component, \(\Phi=P/P^0\) the group of connected components (SGA 3 VIA 4). Then

(i) The natural functor going from the category of group schemes which are extensions (resp. commutative extensions) of \(\Phi\) by \(Z_k\), to the category of group schemes which are extensions (resp. commutative extensions) of \(P\) by \(Z_k\), is an equivalence of categories. In particular, if \(P\) is connected, every extension of \(P\) by \(Z_k\) is trivial, and the category of these extensions is rigid.

(ii) Suppose that \(\Phi\) is a torsion group (e.g. \(P\) of finite type), and \(P\) and \(Z\) commutative. Then the category of extensions of \(P\) by \(Z_k\) is rigid, i.e. every automorphism of an object of this category is trivial. If moreover there exists \(n>0\) such that \(n.\operatorname{id}_\Phi=0\), for example if \(\Phi\) is finite, e.g. \(Z\) of finite type, then the group of classes of commutative extensions of \(P\) by \(G_k\) is given by a canonical isomorphism

\[(5.5.1)\qquad \operatorname{Ext}^1(P,Z_k)\xleftarrow{\sim} \operatorname{Hom}(\Phi,(Z\otimes_{\mathbb Z}\mathbb Q)/Z),\]

given by the coboundary operator of the exact sequence of the \(\operatorname{Ext}\) deduced from the exact sequence

\[(5.5.2)\qquad 0\longrightarrow Z\longrightarrow Z\otimes_{\mathbb Z}\mathbb Q \longrightarrow (Z\otimes_{\mathbb Z}\mathbb Q)/Z\longrightarrow0.\]
Let us note that the canonical morphism
\[P\longrightarrow\Phi\]
evidently induces an isomorphism on the sets of irreducible components, for it is a flat morphism with irreducible fibers,

Hence by virtue of 5.1, the induced functor from the category of torsors under \(Z_\Phi\) to the category of torsors under \(Z_P\) is an equivalence of categories. (Recall that a group scheme locally of finite type over \(k\) is geometrically unibranch). The same conclusion applies to the categories of bitorsors, and to the morphisms
\[P\times P\longrightarrow\Phi\times\Phi,\qquad P\times P\times P\longrightarrow\Phi\times\Phi\times\Phi.\]

The description VII 1.1.6 of the extensions of \(P\) by \(Z_k\) resp. of \(\Phi\) by \(Z_k\) (and VII 1.3 in the case of commutative extensions) then implies the assertions of (i). Let us now restrict ourselves to the case of commutative extensions. Then the group of automorphisms of an extension of \(P\) by \(G_k\) is the group
\[\operatorname{Hom}(P,G_k)\xleftarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Hom}(\Phi,G_k),\]
which is evidently zero if \(\Phi\) is a torsion group. We have similarly, if \(V=G\otimes_{\mathbb Z}\mathbb Q\), and when \(\Phi\) is finite,
\[\operatorname{Ext}^i(\Phi,V_k)=0\quad\text{for all }i\in\mathbb Z,\]
for the groups considered are vector spaces over \(\mathbb Q\) annihilated by the integer \(n>0\). The isomorphism (5.5.1) follows at once.
One proves in essentially the same way:

Corollary 5.6.

Let \(k\) be a field, \(Z\) a torsion-free commutative group, \(P\) and \(Q\) two commutative group schemes locally of finite type over \(k\), \(\Phi=P/P^0\) and \(\Psi=Q/Q^0\) the identity components. Then:

(i) The natural functor, going from the category of biextensions of \((\Phi,\Psi)\) by \(Z_k\) to that of biextensions of \((P,Q)\) by \(Z_k\), is an equivalence of categories. If in particular \(P\) or \(Q\) is connected, the category of biextensions of \((P,Q)\) by \(Z_k\) is equivalent to the punctual category.

(ii) Suppose that \(\Phi\) or \(\Psi\) is a torsion group. Then the category of biextensions of \((P,Q)\) by \(Z_k\) is rigid. If moreover there exists an integer \(n>0\) such that \(n.\operatorname{id}_\Phi=0\) or \(n.\operatorname{id}_\Psi=0\), then the group of classes of biextensions of \((P,Q)\) by \(Z_k\) is given by a canonical isomorphism

\[(5.6.1)\qquad \operatorname{Biext}^1(P,Q;G_k)\xleftarrow{\sim} \operatorname{Hom}(\Phi\otimes\Psi,(Z\otimes_{\mathbb Z}\mathbb Q)/Z),\]

given by the coboundary operator of the exact sequence of the \(\operatorname{Biext}^i\) (\(i=0,1\)) deduced from the exact sequence (5.5.2).

5.7. Let \(S\) be a scheme, \(T\) a closed subscheme, \(Z\) a set. Let us call constant scheme over \(S\), truncated over \(T\), with value \(Z\), and denote by \(Z_{S,T}\), the scheme obtained by gluing, taking the sum of \(Z\) copies \(S_z\) (\(z\in Z\)) of \(S\), which are glued along the open set \(U=S-T\) complementary to \(T\). Thus we have
\[(5.7.1)\qquad Z_{S,T}\times_S U\xleftarrow{\sim}U, \qquad Z_{S,T}\times_S T\xleftarrow{\sim}Z_T,\]
and \(Z_{S,T}\) is a scheme étale over \(S\). For a variable \(S\)-scheme \(S'\), we have an isomorphism functorial in \(S'\):
\[(5.7.2)\qquad \operatorname{Hom}_S(S',Z_{S,T})\xleftarrow{\sim} \operatorname{Hom}_T(S'_T,Z_T),\]
the arrow being defined by "restriction to \(S'_T\)", taking into account the second isomorphism (5.7.1); we leave to the reader the task of verifying that this arrow is bijective. Denoting by
\[i:T\longrightarrow S\]
the inclusion, the functorial isomorphism (5.7.2) can also be written as an isomorphism of sheaves
\[(5.7.3)\qquad Z_{S,T}\xleftarrow{\sim}i_*(Z_T).\]

We see from this formula, or directly from the definition, that for variable \(Z\), \(Z_{S,T}\) depends functorially on \(Z\), the said functor commuting with finite projective limits. In particular it takes groups to groups, commutative groups to commutative groups, which is also clear from the form (2.7.2) of the functor represented by \(Z_{S,T}\).

Let us also point out that it is trivial from the definition, or from (5.7.3), that the formation of \(Z_{S,T}\) commutes with any base change \(f:S'\longrightarrow S\), which gives rise to an isomorphism of functors in \(Z\)
\[(5.7.4)\qquad Z_{S,T}\times_S S'\xleftarrow{\sim}Z_{S',T'}, \qquad\text{where }T'=T\times_S S'=T_{S'}.\]
5.8. We propose to study the torsors \(E\) under a group of the form \(Z_{S,T}\), where \(Z\) is a group, assumed here commutative for simplicity. We have a natural functor
\[(5.8.0)\qquad E\longmapsto E_T\]

from the category of torsors under \(Z_{S,T}\) to the category of torsors under \(Z_T\). I claim that this functor is an equivalence of categories. Indeed, the fact that it is fully faithful, i.e. that for two torsors, \(E,F\) under \(Z_{S,T}\), the map \(\operatorname{Hom}(E,F)\longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}(E_T,F_T)\) is bijective, reduces immediately by fpqc descent to the case where \(E\) and \(F\) are trivial, hence to the case \(E=F=(G_{S,T})\), where it is none other than the bijectivity of (5.7.2) for \(S'=S\). Let us prove that (5.8.0) is essentially surjective. Let \(E_T\) be a torsor under \(Z_T\). We have already noted in the proof of 5.1 that \(E_T\) is representable and locally trivial for the étale topology. It thus suffices to prove that
\[H^1(S_{\mathrm{\acute et}},Z_{S,T})\longrightarrow H^1(T_{\mathrm{\acute et}},Z_T)\]
is bijective, which follows from (5.7.3) and from the relation
\[R^1i_{\mathrm{\acute et}*}(Z_T)=0,\]
a particular case of SGA 4 VIII 5.6.

5.8.1. Let now \(P\) be a commutative group scheme over \(S\), and let us propose to determine the category of extensions of sheaves of commutative groups of \(P\) by \(Z_{S,T}\). For this, we use the description (VII 1.1.6, 1.3) in terms of torsors over \(P\), \(P\times P\), \(P\times P\times P\), under the groups inverse images of \(Z_{S,T}\). Now these inverse images are of the same form as \(Z_{S,T}\) by virtue of (5.7.4), and we can therefore apply to the description of these torsors the preceding result, which tells us that they correspond to the torsors over \(P_T\), \(P_T\times_TP_T\), \(P_T\times_TP_T\times_TP_T\) under the groups inverse images of \(Z_T\). One thus finds:

Proposition 5.9.

Let \(S\) be a scheme, \(T\) a closed subscheme, \(G\) a commutative group, \(G_{S,T}\) the corresponding truncated constant group scheme over \(S\) defined in 5.7, \(P\) a commutative group scheme over \(S\). Then the functor of restriction to \(T\) induces an equivalence between the category of sheaves of commutative groups which are extensions of \(P\) by \(G_{S,T}\), and the category of sheaves of commutative groups which are extensions of \(P_T\) by \(G_T\).
One finds, by an essentially identical proof:

Corollary 5.10.

With the notations of 5.9, let moreover \(Q\) be a commutative group scheme over \(S\). Then the functor of restriction to \(T\) induces an equivalence between the category of biextensions of \((P,Q)\) by \(Z_{S,T}\), and the category of biextensions of \((P_T,Q_T)\) by \(Z_T\).

pages.title: 6. Extensions and biextensions by the Néron model of \(G_m\).

blocks.html: 6. Extensions and biextensions by the Néron model of \(G_m\).

6.1. Let \(S\) be a scheme, \(T\) a closed subscheme, \(Z\) a set, whence a truncated constant scheme \(Z_{S,T}\) over \(S\) (5.7). We have a canonical homomorphism, functorial in \(Z\), of schemes étale over \(S\):
\[(6.1.1)\qquad Z_S\longrightarrow Z_{S,T},\]

which is a surjective morphism, as follows at once from (5.7.1). From its functoriality in \(Z\) it follows that when \(Z\) is a group, (6.1.1) is a homomorphism of group schemes étale over \(S\). Its kernel is therefore a group scheme étale over \(S\), denoted \(Z_{S,U}\), and we have a canonical exact sequence, functorial in \(Z\):
\[(6.1.2)\qquad 0\longrightarrow Z_{S,U}\longrightarrow Z_S \longrightarrow Z_{S,T}\longrightarrow0.\]

One sees at once, thanks to (5.7.1), that \(Z_{S,U}\) is the sum of a family of \(S\)-schemes \(S_n\), \(n\in Z\), with \(S_n=S\) for \(n=0\), and \(S_n=U\) for \(n\ne0\). From this description of \(Z_{S,U}\) it follows easily that for every Group \(F\) over \(S\), the map of restriction to \(U\) induces a bijection:
\[(6.1.3)\qquad \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{gr}}(Z_{S,U},F) \xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{gr}}(Z_U,F|U).\]

6.1.4. We suppose in what follows that \(Z\) is commutative, and we propose to examine the extensions of commutative Groups of \(Z_{S,T}\) by a given commutative Group \(G\) over \(S\). By the exact sequence (6.1.2), the category of these extensions is equivalent to the category of extensions \(F\) of \(Z_S\) by \(G\), equipped with a trivialization of the inverse image of this extension by \(Z_{S,U}\longrightarrow Z_S\). By virtue of (6.1.3), such a trivialization amounts to the datum of a trivialization of the extension \(F|U\) of \(Z_U\) by \(G|U\).

For the sequel, we will take for \(Z\) the group \(\mathbb Z\) of the integers, so \(\mathbb Z_S\) is the free Group on one generator, and VII 1.4 provides us with an equivalence between the category of extensions of \(\mathbb Z_S\) by \(G\), and the category of torsors under \(G\). One thus finds, applying the same result to \(Z_U\) and \(G|U\):

Lemma 6.2.

Let \(S\) be a scheme, \(T\) a closed subscheme, \(U=S-T\), \(G\) a commutative Group over \(S\). The category of commutative Groups over \(S\) which are extensions of \(\mathbb Z_{S,T}\) (defined in (5.7)) by \(G\) is equivalent to the category of pairs \((F,s)\) formed of a torsor \(F\) under \(G\) and a section \(s\) of \(F|U\). If \(\alpha:S\longrightarrow\mathbb Z_{S,T}\) is the section of \(\mathbb Z_{S,T}\) which is the image of the section \(1\) of \(\mathbb Z_S\) over \(S\), to the extension \(\overline G\) of \(\mathbb Z_{S,T}\) by \(G\) corresponds the torsor \(F=\alpha^*(\overline G)\) under \(G\), equipped with the section of \(F|U\) given by the restriction to \(U\) of the unit section of \(\overline G\).

6.2.1. If \(\overline G\) corresponds to the pair \((F,s)\), the inverse image \(f_n^*(\overline G)\) in \(\overline G\) of the section \(f_n\) of \(\mathbb Z_{S,T}\), defined by the constant section with value \(n\in\mathbb Z\) of \(\mathbb Z_S\), is canonically identified, as a torsor under \(G\), with the n-th power \(F^{(n)}\) of the \(G\)-torsor \(F\); the canonical isomorphism of \(G_U\)-torsors \(f_n^*(\overline G)|U\xrightarrow{\sim}G_U\), defined by the first relation (5.7.1), is defined by the section \(s^{(n)}\) of \(F_U^{(n)}\), the n-th power of the given section \(s\) of \(F_U\). From this explicit description follows the following description of the sections of \(\overline G\) over \(S\): a section \(\bar g\) of \(\overline G\) over \(S\) is defined by

1. a) a section \(n\) of \(\mathbb Z_{S,T}\) over \(S\), which can be interpreted as a section of \(\mathbb Z_T\) i.e. a locally constant function on \(T\) with values in \(\mathbb Z\), or if one prefers, a partition \(T=\coprod_{i\in\mathbb Z}T_i\) of \(T\) into a disjoint sum of open parts indexed by the \(i\in\mathbb Z\);
  2. b) a family of sections \[\bar g_i\in\Gamma(F^{(i)}/S'_i),\quad i\in\mathbb Z,\quad\text{where }S'_i=S-T'_i,\quad T'_i=\coprod_{\substack{j\in\mathbb Z\\j\ne i}}T_j,\] the sections \(\bar g_i\) for \(i\in\mathbb Z\) being linked by the following compatibility condition: for \(i,j\in\mathbb Z\), \(g_i\) and \(g_j\) coincide on \(S'_i\cap S'_j=U\), when we identify \(F^{(i)}|U\) and \(F^{(j)}|U\) with \(G_U\), thanks to the section \(s^{(i)}\) resp. \(s^{(j)}\) of these torsors. In other words, there must exist a section \(h_{ij}\in\Gamma(G/U)\), evidently uniquely determined, such that we have \[\bar g_i=h_{ij}s^{(i)},\qquad \bar g_j=h_{ij}s^{(j)}.\]

Since the description 3.2 of an extension \(\overline G\) of \(\mathbb Z_{S,T}\) by \(G\) is evidently compatible with any base change \(S'\longrightarrow S\), the preceding gives a complete description of the sheaf \(\overline G\) as a functor in the argument \(S'\in\operatorname{Ob}\operatorname{Sch}/S\).

6.2.2. When \(T\) is a scheme which is a sum of non-empty connected schemes \(T_i\), \(i\in I\), then the description 6.2.1 of the sections of \(\overline G\) over \(S\) can be simplified: such a section is defined by a family of integers \((n_i)_{i\in I}\) indexed by the same set \(I\), a family of sections \[\bar g_i\in\Gamma(F^{(n_i)}/S'_i),\quad\text{for }i\in I,\quad\text{where }S'_i=S-T'_i,\quad T'_i=\coprod_{\substack{j\in I\\j\ne i}}T_j,\] the \(\bar g_i\) satisfying the following compatibility condition: for every pair of elements \(i,j\in I\), the restrictions of \(\bar g_i\) and \(\bar g_j\) to \(S'_i\cap S'_j=U\) are identified, modulo the identification of \(F^{(n_i)}|U\) and of \(F^{(n_j)}|U\), deduced from the sections \(s^{(n_i)}\) resp. \(s^{(n_j)}\) of these torsors.

6.2.3. When finally we suppose \(T\) connected and non-empty, the datum of a section \(\bar g\) of \(\overline G\) over \(S\) amounts to the datum of an integer \(n\), and of a section \(\bar g\) of \(F^{(n)}\) over \(S\). There is no longer any compatibility condition to impose, so that the section \(s\) of \(F|U\) is absent from the description of the group of sections of \(\overline G\) over \(S\). (But \(s\) appears again, of course, as soon as we propose to give a description of the points of \(\overline G\) with values in a general \(S\)-scheme \(S'\).)

6.2.4. Since \(\overline G\) is the union of the "open sets" \(f_n^*(\overline G)\) (\(n\in\mathbb Z\)) described in 6.2.1, we conclude that \(\overline G\) is representable if and only if the torsors \(F^{(n)}\) are. It suffices for this, for example, that \(G\) be representable and be an affine group scheme over \(S\), thanks to descent theory SGA 1 VIII 2.1.

6.3. In the sequel, we will suppose that \(G=G_{m,S}\), so that every extension \(\overline G\) of \(\mathbb Z_{S,T}\) by \(G=G_{mS}\) will indeed be representable (6.2.4), and of course, will be smooth over \(S\) as an extension of an étale group scheme \(\mathbb Z_{S,T}\) by a smooth group scheme \(G_{mS}\). Moreover, the datum of a torsor \(F\) under \(G_m\) is equivalent to the datum of an invertible Module \(L\) on \(S\) (by associating to the latter the torsor of invertible sections of \(L\)). Hence \(\overline G\) is described by a pair \((L,s)\), where \(L\) is an invertible Module on \(S\), and \(s\) a trivialization of \(L\) over \(U=S-T\), i.e. an invertible section of \(L|U\). When \(U\) is schematically dense in \(S\), and \(S\) is locally noetherian, or reduced with the set of its irreducible components locally finite, the datum of such a pair is equivalent to that of a divisor \(D\) on \(S\) such that
\[(6.3.1)\qquad \operatorname{supp}D\subset T,\]
by making correspond to such a divisor the pair
\[(6.3.2)\qquad (\mathcal O_S(-D),s_D|U)\]

of the invertible Module \(\mathcal O_S(-D)\) associated to \(-D\) (EGA IV 21.2.8.1), and of the restriction to \(U\) of the canonical rational section \(s_{-D}\) of the latter (EGA IV 21.2.11 and 20.2.11 (iii)). In any case, without hypothesis on \(S\), the datum of a divisor \(D\) on \(S\) satisfying (6.3.1) defines a pair \((L,s)\) by (6.3.2), hence an extension \(\overline G\) of \(\mathbb Z_{S,T}\) by \(G_m\), which we will call the Néron–Raynaud model of \(G_m\), associated to the divisor \(D\) and to the closed set \(T\) of \(S\) satisfying (6.3.1). If \(T=\operatorname{supp}D\), we omit \(T\) from the terminology.

It is immediate that the formation of this extension \(\overline G\) commutes with any base change \(f:S'\longrightarrow S\) such that the inverse image divisor \(f^*(D)\) is defined (EGA IV 21.4.2), in particular with any flat base change.

6.3.3. One sees at once, thanks to the general description 6.2.1 of the sections of \(\overline G\), that these can be identified with the sections \(f\) of the sheaf \(\mathcal M_S^*\) of regular meromorphic sections on \(S\) (EGA IV 20.1.8) whose divisor \(\operatorname{div}(f)\) is locally (in the sense of the Zariski topology) an integer multiple of \(D\). If \(V\) is an open subset of \(S\) such that the restriction of \(\operatorname{div}f\) to \(V\) equals that of \(nD\), where \(n\in\mathbb Z\), then the section of \(\mathbb Z_{S,T}\) defined by \(f\) is none other than the image of the constant section with value \(n\) of \(\mathbb Z_S\) in (6.1.2). The preceding description of the group of sections of \(\overline G\) over \(S\) remains valid over any open subset of \(S\), more generally for the group of points of \(\overline G\) with values in any flat \(S\)-scheme \(S'\). Since \(\overline G\) itself is flat (and even smooth) over \(S\), this description thus gives again a complete characterization of the Néron–Raynaud model of \(G_m\) defined by \(D\) and \(T\supset\operatorname{supp}D\).

6.4. Suppose now that \(S\) is normal, with the set of its irreducible components locally finite (so \(S\) is a scheme which is a sum of integral normal schemes), and suppose that the divisor \(D\) is positive, with multiplicities 1 (EGA IV 21.6), that \(T=\operatorname{supp}D\), and that \(T\) is locally irreducible, i.e. that its irreducible components are disjoint. Then for every rational function \(f\) on \(S\), regular on \(U=S-T\), \(\operatorname{div}f\) is locally a multiple of \(D\), so we have in this case a canonical isomorphism of groups:
\[(6.4.1)\qquad \Gamma(S,\overline G)\xrightarrow{\sim}\Gamma(U,G_{mU}).\]

Suppose even that \(T\) is geometrically unibranch and locally integral, a hypothesis which is stable under smooth base change (EGA IV 17.5.7). Then, all the hypotheses being stable under base change, we deduce from (6.4.1) that for \(S'\) smooth over \(S\), we have a canonical isomorphism of groups, functorial in \(S'\):
\[(6.4.2)\qquad \operatorname{Hom}_S(S',\overline G)\xrightarrow{\sim} \operatorname{Hom}_U(S'_U,G_{mU})\simeq\Gamma(S'_U,\mathcal O_{S'_U}^*).\]

Since \(\overline G\) is itself smooth over \(S\), this isomorphism characterizes the group scheme \(\overline G\) over \(S\) up to unique isomorphism. On the other hand, it shows that \(\overline G\) plays the role of a "Néron model" for the group scheme \(G_{mU}\) over the open subset \(U\) of \(S\), which (together with the fact that it was introduced and used for the first time by M. RAYNAUD) justifies the terminology adopted in 6.3 to designate \(\overline G\).

Theorem 6.5.

Let \(S\) be a locally integral normal scheme, \(D\) a positive divisor on \(S\), without multiple components, such that the support of \(D\) is geometrically unibranch and locally integral. Let \(\overline G\) be the Néron–Raynaud model of \(G_m\) over \(S\) relative to \(D\) (6.3), and consider the category of torsors \(E\) over \(S\) under the group scheme \(\overline G\). The restriction functor

\[E\longmapsto E_U=E|U,\]

which goes from this category to the category of torsors over \(U\) under the group scheme \(G_{mU}\), is fully faithful. If \(S\) is a regular scheme (more generally, if its local rings are factorial), then this functor is even an equivalence of categories.
Let us show that the functor in question is fully faithful, i.e. that for two torsors \(E,F\) under \(\overline G\), the restriction map
\[(*)\qquad \operatorname{Hom}(E,F)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Hom}(E_U,F_U)\]

is bijective, where the Hom denote the sets of homomorphisms of torsors. Let us note first that this question is manifestly local on \(S\) for the Zariski topology: if \(S\) is a union of open sets \(S_i\) such that for every open set \(S'\) of \(S\) contained in some \(S_i\) (hence in particular for \(S'\) of the form \(S_i\) or \(S_i\cap S_j\)), the map
\[\operatorname{Hom}(E_{S'},F_{S'})\longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}(E_{S'\cap U},F_{S'\cap U})\]

is bijective, then the map (*) is bijective. On the other hand, it follows at once from 5.8 applied to \(S,T,\mathbb Z\), and from 5.1 applied to \(T,\mathbb Z\), that every torsor under \(\overline G\) is locally trivial for the Zariski topology. These two observations reduce us to proving the bijectivity of the map (*) in the case where \(E=F=\overline G\). But then this map is none other than (6.4.1), whose bijectivity has already been established.

Let us now show that the functor \(E\longmapsto E_U\) in question is essentially surjective when the local rings of \(S\) are factorial. Since \(G_{m,S}\) is identified with an open subgroup scheme of \(\overline G\), it suffices to prove that every torsor under \(G_{m,U}\) is isomorphic to the restriction to \(U\) of a torsor under \(G_{m,S}\). It amounts to the same to say that every invertible Module on \(U\) is isomorphic to the restriction of an invertible Module on \(S\), which indeed follows from the hypothesis made on \(S\) (EGA IV 21.6.11, where one restricts to the locally noetherian case, which is the only case that will serve us in the sequel). This proves 6.5.

6.5.1. Let us now place ourselves in the case where we suppose moreover that \(S\) is locally noetherian and regular. Since \(S\) is then locally factorial, the datum of \(D\) is equivalent to that of a closed subscheme (which we again denote \(D\)) which is purely 1-codimensional, reduced and geometrically unibranch. Let us also note that the hypotheses on \(S\) and \(D\) are stable under any smooth base change \(S'\longrightarrow S\), so that the conclusion of 6.5 will apply to \(S'\) equipped with the subscheme \(D'=D\times_SS'\) of \(S'\). This said, the description VII 1.1.6 of the extension Groups gives us at once the following consequence:

Corollary 6.6.

Let \(S\) be a locally noetherian and regular scheme, \(D\) a reduced closed subscheme, geometrically unibranch and purely 1-codimensional (hence defining a divisor on \(S\)), \(\overline G\) the Néron–Raynaud model of \(G_m\) relative to \(D\), \(P\) a commutative group scheme smooth over \(S\). Consider the category of commutative extensions of sheaves of Groups of \(P\) by \(\overline G\). Then the functor of restriction to \(U\), \(E\longmapsto E|U\), induces an equivalence of this category with the category of extensions of \(P_U\) by \(G_{m,U}\).

6.6.1. One obtains by the same method the analogous statement, relative to the case of not necessarily commutative extensions (\(P\) no longer being assumed commutative). We leave to the reader the details of the proof, slightly more complicated by the fact that one must here consider bitorsors instead of torsors.
By essentially the same proof as 6.6, one obtains likewise:

Corollary 6.7.

Let \(S,D,\overline G\) be as in 6.6, and \(P,Q\) two commutative group schemes smooth over \(S\). We consider the category of biextensions of \((P,Q)\) by \(\overline G\). Then the functor of restriction to \(U\), \(E\longmapsto E|U\), which goes from this category to the category of biextensions of \((P_U,Q_U)\) by \(G_{m,U}\), is an equivalence of categories.

Remarks 6.8. a) We will apply 6.6 and 6.7 when \(S\) is a trait, and \(D\) the divisor defined by the inclusion of the closed point \(s\) of \(S\) in \(S\). In this case, \(U\) is the spectrum of the field of fractions \(K\) of \(S\), and \(\overline G\) is the usual Néron model of the multiplicative group over \(K\), introduced by Raynaud. It will be noted that, even restricting to this case, the proof of 6.6 and of 6.7 uses 6.5 over bases more general than traits (such as \(P\) and its cartesian powers ...). Nevertheless, it suffices in this case to know the existence of the Néron model \(\overline G\) in the particular case considered, in order to deduce formally 6.5 for base schemes smooth over \(S\), hence to obtain 6.6 and 6.7. For us, the introduction of \(\overline G\) will above all be a convenient intermediary for studying, in the next section, the problem of prolongations of extensions and of biextensions by the multiplicative group.

b) One can define a Néron–Raynaud model \(\overline G\) of \(G_{m,U}\), satisfying the fundamental condition (6.4.2) for every \(S'\) smooth over \(S\), under conditions distinctly more general than those considered above, \(U\) being simply a schematically dense open subset of a locally noetherian normal scheme \(S\). In this case, \(\overline G\) will be a sheaf for the fppf topology, not necessarily representable. Denoting by \(\underline D\) the sheaf, on the small étale site of \(S\), of divisors on \(S\) with support contained in \(T=S-U\), and (by abuse of language) by the same letter the canonical extension of \(\underline D\) to a sheaf on \((\operatorname{Sch})/S\) (whose sections over the relative scheme \(f:S'\longrightarrow S\) are the sections of \(f_{\mathrm{et}}^*(\underline D)\) over \(S'\)), the sought-for model is an extension of Groups
\[(6.8.1)\qquad 0\longrightarrow G_{mS}\longrightarrow\overline G \longrightarrow\underline D\longrightarrow0.\]

The construction of a canonical extension (6.8.1) (without normality hypothesis on \(S\)) presents no difficulties, using the general description VII 1.1.6, and one obtains at the same time (6.4.2) for \(S'\) étale over \(S\). To have the same relation for every \(S'\) smooth over \(S\), it amounts to the same to verify that the formation of the sheaf \(\underline D\) commutes with any smooth base change \(f:S'\longrightarrow S\), which for \(S\) normal follows from EGA IV Err. 53, 21.4.9.
6.9. "Self-criticism" (observations of P. DELIGNE)*.
The nos 5 and 6 are really not very inspiring. One periodically has the impression of redoing "by hand" the formalism \(i_*,i^*\), without seeing what is geometric or "general non-sense".
I propose to bring out the "geometric" statements a)b)c):

a) (cf. 6.8 b) Let \(j:V\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow S\) be a schematically dense open subset of a noetherian normal scheme \(S\). The étale sheaf \(G_{mS}\) is then identified with a subsheaf of the étale sheaf \(j_*G_{mU}\). Let \(\underline D\) be the quotient étale sheaf
\[0\longrightarrow G_{mS}\longrightarrow j_*G_{mS}\longrightarrow\underline D\longrightarrow0.\]
Then the formation of \(\underline D\) commutes with any smooth base change (EGA ErrIV 53, 21.4.9); moreover, \(R^1j_*G_m=0\) (Th. 90) if the strictly local rings of \(S\) are factorial.
b) If \(U=S-Y\), with \(Y\) a geometrically unibranch divisor, and if \(i\) is the inclusion of \(Y\) in \(S\), then
\[\underline D=i_*\mathbb Z_Y.\]

c) If \(Y\) is a closed subscheme of a scheme \(X\), and \(E\) a set, then the étale sheaf \(i_*E_Y\) is representable by a scheme étale over \(X\), whose formation is compatible with any base change.

Corollary.

Let \(S\) be a noetherian normal scheme, \(Y\) a geometrically unibranch divisor and \(U=S-Y\):

\[U\mathop{\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow}^{j}S\mathop{\longleftarrow\joinrel\lhook}^{i}Y.\]

There exists a unique smooth group scheme \(\mathcal G_m\) over \(S\), such that after any smooth base change, we have \(\mathcal G_m|_{S'_{\mathrm{et}}}=j_*G_{mU}\).
The preceding requirements indeed determine \(\mathcal G_m\) as a sheaf (for the étale topology) on the category of schemes smooth over \(S\). By a), b), the sequence
\[0\longrightarrow G_{mS}\longrightarrow\mathcal G_m \mathop{\longrightarrow}^{q}i_*\mathbb Z_Y\longrightarrow0\]
is exact. By c), \(i_*\mathbb Z_Y\) is representable. Finally the morphism \(q\) is representable.
The use of the preceding "small smooth site" should make it possible to deduce formally the statements of section 6 from a).
