pages.title: 7. Canonical prolongations of extensions and of biextensions by \(G_m\)

blocks.html: 7. Canonical prolongations of extensions and of biextensions by \(G_m\)

7.0. We now reap the fruit of our preparations of nos 5 and 6. Let us place ourselves first in the case of a base scheme \(S\) which is a trait, i.e. the spectrum of a discrete valuation ring, and let \(\eta\) be its generic point, \(s\) its closed point, \(k=k(s)\). Let \(P\) be a commutative group scheme smooth over \(S\); we will set \(P_o=P_s\), the special fiber of \(P\). In the present section, we consider only commutative groups, and in particular the extensions of groups considered are commutative.

Theorem 7.1.

The notations are those recalled in 7.0.

a) Suppose that the group of connected components of the special fiber of \(P\),

\[\Phi=P_o/P_o^o\]

is a torsion group, — which is the case in particular if \(P\) is of finite type over \(S\); then the functor \(E\longmapsto E_\eta\) from the category of extensions of \(P\) by \(G_{mS}\) to the category of extensions of \(P_\eta\) by \(G_{m\eta}\) is fully faithful. Suppose that there exists an integer \(n>0\) such that \(n\Phi=0\); then if \(E_\eta\) is an extension of \(P_\eta\) by \(G_{m\eta}\), there is associated to it a canonical homomorphism

\[(7.1.1)\qquad d(E_\eta):\Phi\longrightarrow(\mathbb Q/\mathbb Z)_k,\]

whose vanishing is necessary and sufficient for the extension \(E_\eta\) to be isomorphic to the generic fiber of an extension of \(P\) by \(G_{mS}\). In particular, if \(P_o\) is connected, the preceding functor \(E\longmapsto E_\eta\) is an equivalence of categories.

b) Let \(P\) and \(Q\) be two group schemes smooth over \(S\); let us set

\[\Phi=P_o/P_o^o,\qquad \Psi=Q_o/Q_o^o,\]

and suppose that \(\Phi\) or \(\Psi\) is a torsion group (for example \(P\) or \(Q\) of finite type over \(S\)); then the functor \(E\longmapsto E_\eta\) from the category of biextensions of \((P,Q)\) by \(G_{mS}\) to the category of biextensions of \((P_\eta,Q_\eta)\) by \(G_{m\eta}\) is fully faithful. Suppose that there exists an integer \(n>0\) such that \(n\Phi=0\) or \(n\Psi=0\); then if \(E_\eta\) is a biextension of \((P_\eta,Q_\eta)\) by \(G_{m\eta}\), there is associated to it a canonical pairing

\[(7.1.2)\qquad d(E_\eta):\Phi\otimes_{\mathbb Z}\Psi\longrightarrow(\mathbb Q/\mathbb Z)_k,\]

whose vanishing is necessary and sufficient for \(E\) to be isomorphic to the generic fiber of a biextension \(E\) of \((P,Q)\) by \(G_{mS}\). In particular, if \(P_o\) or \(Q_o\) is connected, the preceding functor \(E\longmapsto E_\eta\) is an equivalence of categories.

Proof. a) Let \(\overline G\) be the Néron–Raynaud model of \(G_m\). The functor in question is the composite of the functor "extension of the kernel \(G_{mS}\longrightarrow\overline G\)" with the functor "restriction to \(\eta\)" defined on the category of biextensions by \(\overline G\). The latter functor being an equivalence of categories by virtue of 6.6, we are reduced to studying the first functor. For this, we use the exact sequence of group schemes
\[(7.1.3)\qquad 0\longrightarrow G_{mS}\longrightarrow\overline G\longrightarrow\mathbb Z_{S,s}\longrightarrow0,\]

the notation for \(\mathbb Z_{S,s}\) being that of 5.7. The exact sequence of the \(\operatorname{Ext}^i\) (\(i=0,1\)) associated to this exact sequence shows us that for the functor in question to be fully faithful, it is necessary and sufficient that we have
\[(7.1.4)\qquad \operatorname{Hom}(P,\mathbb Z_{S,s})=0,\]
and that the obstruction, for an extension \(\overline E\) of \(P\) by \(\overline G\), to come from an extension \(E\) of \(P\) by \(G_{mS}\) lies in
\[(7.1.5)\qquad \operatorname{Ext}^1(P,\mathbb Z_{S,s}).\]

Now the first group (7.1.4) is isomorphic to \(\operatorname{Hom}(P_o,\mathbb Z_k)\) by virtue of (5.7.2), which is itself isomorphic to \(\operatorname{Hom}(\Phi,\mathbb Z_k)\), so that (7.1.4) is equivalent to the relation
\[(7.1.4\ \mathrm{bis})\qquad \operatorname{Hom}(\Phi,\mathbb Z_k)=0.\]
This relation is certainly verified if \(\Phi\) is a torsion group. On the other hand, if we have even \(n\Phi=0\), then by virtue of 5.9 and 5.5 we have canonical isomorphisms

\[(7.1.6)\qquad \operatorname{Ext}^1(P,\mathbb Z_{S,s})\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow} \operatorname{Ext}^1(P_o,\mathbb Z_k)\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}(\Phi,(\mathbb Q/\mathbb Z)_k),\]
which completes the proof of 7.1 a).

b) The proof is essentially the same. We are reduced, thanks to 6.7, to studying the functor "extension of the kernel \(G_{mS}\longrightarrow\overline G\)" from the category of biextensions of \((P,Q)\) by \(G_{mS}\) to that of biextensions of \((P,Q)\) by \(\overline G\). This study is carried out using the exact sequence (7.1.3), and the associated exact sequence of the \(\operatorname{Biext}^i\) (\(i=0,1\)) (VII 3.7.5). The full faithfulness of the functor in question is equivalent to the relation
\[(7.1.7)\qquad \operatorname{Hom}(P\otimes Q,\mathbb Z_{S,s})=0,\]

now the first member is isomorphic to \(\operatorname{Hom}(P_o\otimes Q_o,\mathbb Z_k)\) by virtue of (5.7.2), hence to \(\operatorname{Hom}(\Phi\otimes\Psi,\mathbb Z_k)\), a group which is indeed zero if \(\Phi\) or \(\Psi\) is a torsion group. On the other hand, for a given biextension \(\overline E\) of \((P,Q)\) by \(\overline G\), the obstruction to its coming from a biextension \(E\) of \((P,Q)\) by \(G_{mS}\) lies in \(\operatorname{Biext}^1(P,Q;\mathbb Z_{S,s})\), which is isomorphic by virtue of 5.10 to \(\operatorname{Biext}^1(P_o,Q_o,\mathbb Z_k)\), itself isomorphic (if \(n\Phi=0\) or \(n\Psi=0\)) to \(\operatorname{Hom}(\Phi\otimes\Psi,\mathbb Q/\mathbb Z)\) by virtue of 5.6. This completes the proof of 7.1.

Remark 7.2. One may propose, given a smooth group scheme \(P\) over a locally noetherian regular connected scheme \(S\) with generic point \(\eta\), to study the functor \(E\longmapsto E_\eta\) from the category of extensions of \(P\) by \(G_{mS}\) to that of extensions of \(P_\eta\) by \(G_{m\eta}\). This problem in fact reduces easily to the one treated in 7.1 a). Suppose for simplicity that \(P\) is of finite type over \(S\). Then it follows easily from 7.1 that the functor in question is fully faithful*. One deduces easily from this that if \(E_\eta\) is an extension of \(P_\eta\) by \(G_{m\eta}\), then for it to be isomorphic to the generic fiber of an extension \(E\) of \(P\) by \(G_{mS}\), it is necessary and sufficient that for every \(s\in S\) such that \(\dim\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_{S,s})=1\), the analogous prolongation problem obtained by the base change \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_{S,s})\longrightarrow S\) have a solution, — which, by virtue of 7.1 a), is expressed by the vanishing of a certain homomorphism
\[(7.2.1)\qquad d(E_\eta,s):P_s/P_s^o\longrightarrow(\mathbb Q/\mathbb Z)_{k(s)}.\]
The preceding assertion reduces in an evident way to the following: let \(U\) be an open subset of \(S\), with complement \(T\) such that
\[\operatorname{codim}(T,S)\geq2,\]

then the functor \(E\longmapsto E|U\) from the category of extensions of \(P\) by \(G_{mS}\) to that of extensions of \(P_U\) by \(G_{mU}\) is an equivalence of categories. Now this assertion is essentially trivial in terms of the description VII 1.1.6, using the fact that for every smooth \(S\)-scheme \(X\) (such as \(P,P\times P,P\times P\times P\ldots\)), \(X\) is locally factorial and \(X_T=X-X_U\) is of codimension \(\geq2\) in \(X\), whence it follows that the functor \(F\longmapsto F|X_U\) from the category of torsors under \(G_{mX}\) to that of torsors under \(G_{mX_U}\) is an equivalence of categories.
One has entirely analogous results for the question of prolongations of biextensions, which we leave to the reader to formulate.

7.3. We are going to make explicit some functorial properties of the obstructions defined in (7.1.1) and (7.1.2) for variable \(P,Q,S\), and of their relations among themselves. We will suppose verified in what follows the conditions which allow these obstructions to be defined. As the verifications present no difficulty, we leave them to the care of the reader.

7.3.1. Let \(u:P'\longrightarrow P\) be a morphism of group schemes smooth over \(S\), \(E_\eta\) an extension of \(P_\eta\) by \(G_{m\eta}\), whence by inverse image by \(u_\eta:P'_\eta\longrightarrow P_\eta\) an extension \(E'_\eta\) of \(P'_\eta\) by \(G_{m\eta}\). This granted, we have commutativity in the diagram
\[(7.3.1.1)\qquad \xymatrix{P'_o/P'^{o}_o\ar[d]_{u_o}\ar[dr]^{d(E'_\eta)}&\\ P_o/P^o_o\ar[r]_{d(E_\eta)}&(\mathbb Q/\mathbb Z)_k.}\]
Likewise, if we have morphisms of group schemes smooth over \(S\)
\[u:P'\longrightarrow P,\qquad v:Q'\longrightarrow Q\]

and a biextension \(E_\eta\) of \((P_\eta,Q_\eta)\) by \(G_{m\eta}\), whence by inverse image by \((u_\eta,v_\eta)\) a biextension \(E'_\eta\) of \((P'_\eta,Q'_\eta)\) by \(G_{m\eta}\), we have commutativity in the diagram
\[(7.3.1.2)\qquad \xymatrix{P'_o/P'^{o}_o\otimes Q'_o/Q'^{o}_o\ar[d]_{u_o\otimes v_o}\ar[dr]^{d(E'_\eta)}&\\ P_o/P^o_o\otimes Q_o/Q^o_o\ar[r]_{d(E_\eta)}&(\mathbb Q/\mathbb Z)_k.}\]
The verification, essentially mechanical, of these compatibilities is left to the reader.

7.3.2. From the compatibility (7.3.1.1) it follows that if \(E_\eta\) is an extension of \(P_\eta\) by \(G_m\), there exists a largest subgroup scheme \(V\) of \(P\) such that the restriction of \(E_\eta\) to \(V_\eta\) extends to an extension of \(V\) by \(G_{mS}\): it is the open subgroup \(V\) of \(P\) containing \(P_\eta\), defined by the condition
\[(7.3.2.1)\qquad V_o/V^o_o=\operatorname{Ker}d(E_\eta).\]

If then \(u:P'\longrightarrow P\) is a morphism of group schemes smooth over \(S\), the extension \(E'_\eta\), inverse image of \(E_\eta\) by \(u_\eta\), prolongs to an extension of \(P'\) by \(G_{mS}\) if and only if we have
\[(7.3.2.2)\qquad u(P')\subset V.\]

In the case of a biextension \(E\) of \((P_\eta,Q_\eta)\) by \(G_{m\eta}\), there exists likewise a largest subgroup scheme \(V\) of \(P\) such that the restriction of \(E\) to \((V_\eta,Q_\eta)\) prolongs to a biextension of \((V,Q)\) by \(G_{mS}\): it is the open subgroup of \(P\), containing the generic fiber \(P_\eta\), defined by the condition that \(V_o/V^o_o\) be the subgroup of \(\Phi\) left annihilator of the pairing (7.1.2), i.e. kernel of the corresponding homomorphism from \(\Phi\) to \(\operatorname{Hom}(\Psi,(\mathbb Q/\mathbb Z)_k)\):
\[(7.3.2.3)\qquad V_o/V^o_o=\operatorname{Ker}\bigl(\Phi\xrightarrow{d(E_\eta)} \operatorname{Hom}(\Psi,\mathbb Q/\mathbb Z)_k\bigr).\]

If then we are given \(u:P'\longrightarrow P\) of group schemes smooth over \(S\), the biextension of \((P'_\eta,Q_\eta)\) by \(G_{m\eta}\) inverse image of \(E_\eta\) by \(u_\eta\) prolongs to a biextension of \((P',Q)\) by \(G_{mS}\) if and only if we have the relation (7.3.2.2). One has symmetric results on exchanging the roles of \(P\) and of \(Q\). But of course one cannot in general find a largest pair of subgroups \(P'\longrightarrow P,Q'\longrightarrow Q\) such that the restriction \(E'_\eta\) of \(E_\eta\) to a biextension of \((P'_\eta,Q'_\eta)\) by \(G_{m\eta}\) prolongs to a biextension of \((P',Q')\) by \(G_{mS}\); one can only say that for a given pair \((P',Q')\), more generally for a given pair of morphisms \(P'\longrightarrow P,Q'\longrightarrow Q\) of smooth group schemes, the sought-for prolongation exists if and only if the restriction of the form \(d(E_\eta)\) to \(P'_o/P'^{o}_o\otimes Q'_o/Q'^{o}_o\) is zero.

7.3.3. Symmetry. Let \(E_\eta\) be a biextension of \((P_\eta,Q_\eta)\) by \(G_{m\eta}\), and consider the symmetric biextension \({}^sE_\eta\) (VII 2.7), which is a biextension of \((Q_\eta,P_\eta)\) by \(G_{m\eta}\). We then have commutativity in the diagram
\[(7.3.3.1)\qquad \xymatrix{\Phi\otimes\Psi\ar[d]_{\mathrm{sym}}\ar[dr]^{d(E_\eta)}&\\ \Psi\otimes\Phi\ar[r]_{d({}^sE_\eta)}&(\mathbb Q/\mathbb Z)_k,}\]

where the vertical arrow is the canonical symmetry. (One may compare this statement with the antisymmetry statement of 2.2.10.) One deduces from it in particular that if \(P=Q\), and if \(E_\eta\) is a symmetric biextension of \((P_\eta,Q_\eta)\) by \(G_{m\eta}\), i.e. if \(E_\eta\) is isomorphic to \({}^sE_\eta\), then the bilinear form \(d(E_\eta)\) on \(\Phi\times\Phi\) with values in \((\mathbb Q/\mathbb Z)_k\) is symmetric (and not alternating as in 2.2.12!). It will be noted that if it is zero, i.e. if \(E\) prolongs to a biextension of \((P,P)\) by \(G_{mS}\), the latter is necessarily symmetric as well, since by virtue of 7.1 a) every isomorphism \({}^sE_\eta\longrightarrow E_\eta\) prolongs in a unique way to an isomorphism \({}^sE\longrightarrow E\).
7.3.4. Reduction of the obstructions of type (7.1.2) to those of type (7.1.1).
Suppose to fix ideas that there exists an integer \(n>0\) such that \(n\Phi=0\), and let

be a section of \(Q\). We denote, by abuse of notation, by \(q_o\in\Psi(k)\) the image in \(\Psi(k)\) of the value of \(q\) at \(s\). Considering \(E_\eta\) as an extension of \(P_{\eta,Q_\eta}\) by \(G_{mQ_\eta}\), we find an extension \(q^*_\eta(E_\eta)\), an extension of \(P_\eta\) by \(G_{m\eta}\), whence an obstruction
\[(7.3.4.1)\qquad d_q\overset{\mathrm{dfn}}=d(q^*_\eta(E_\eta)):\Phi\longrightarrow(\mathbb Q/\mathbb Z)_k.\]
This granted, we have the relation (where we have set \(d=d(E_\eta)\))
valid for every point \(x\) of \(\Phi\) with values in a \(k\)-scheme \(T\).

7.3.4.3. This formula, which for every \(q\in Q(S)\) determines \(d_q\) in terms of the pairing \(d\), evidently gives back the latter in terms of the invariants \(d_q\), at least when every point of \(\Psi\) comes from a section of \(Q\). This condition is moreover verified automatically when \(S\) is strictly local, by virtue of Hensel's lemma. As the pairing \(d\) is known when it is known over the separable closure of \(k\), and its formation commutes with étale localization on \(S\) by virtue of 7.3.5.3 below, we see that, up to passing to the strict localization of \(S\), the determination of the pairing \(d\) reduces to that of the invariants \(d_q\). In particular, when every point of \(\Psi\) comes from a section \(q\) of \(Q\), we see that \(d\) is zero (i.e. \(E_\eta\) prolongs to a biextension \(E\) of \((P,Q)\) by \(G_{mS}\)) if and only if for every section \(q\) of \(Q\), \(d_q\) is zero, i.e. \(q^*_\eta(E_\eta)\) prolongs to an extension of \(P\) by \(G_{mS}\). When one makes no hypothesis on \(\Psi\), the analogous criterion is valid, provided one takes for \(q\) points with values in \(S'\) local étale over \(S\).

7.3.4.4. Conversely, starting from the situation of 7.1 a), the obstruction (7.1.1) can be interpreted as an obstruction of type (7.1.2), by taking \(Q=\mathbb Z_S\) and the biextension of \((P,\mathbb Z_S)\) deduced from \(E\). To see this, it suffices to apply the preceding by taking for \(q\) the section 1 of \(\mathbb Z_S\).
7.3.5. Effect of a base change \(S'\longrightarrow S\). We suppose given a dominant local morphism of traits
\[f:S'\longrightarrow S,\]

and we denote by \(\eta'\) the generic point of \(S'\), by \(s'\) its closed point, and we set \(k'=k(s')\). If \(S=\operatorname{Spec}(V)\), \(S'=\operatorname{Spec}(V')\), \(V'\) is thus a discrete valuation ring dominating the discrete valuation ring \(V\). Let us set
\[(7.3.5.1)\qquad e(S'/S)=e(V'/V)=\operatorname{long}(V'/\mathfrak mV'),\]

where \(\mathfrak m\) is the maximal ideal of \(V\). Let now \(P\) be as in 7.1 a), \(E_\eta\) an extension of \(P_\eta\) by \(G_{m\eta}\), \(P'=P\times_S S'\), \(E'_{\eta'}\) the extension of \(P'_{\eta'}\) by \(G_{m\eta'}\) inverse image of \(E_\eta\). We then have a canonical isomorphism
\[\Phi'\overset{\mathrm{dfn}}=P'_{o}/P'^{o}_{o}\simeq\Phi\otimes_k k'\simeq\Phi_{k'},\]
with the notations of 4.1 a), and we can consequently consider
\[d(E_\eta)_{k'}:\Phi'\longrightarrow(\mathbb Q/\mathbb Z)_{k'}.\]
This granted, we have the relation
Likewise, under the conditions of 7.1 b), and with the evident notations, we have the relation (7.3.5.2), where \(d(E_\eta)\) now denotes the pairing (7.1.2), \(d(E_\eta)_{k'}\) the pairing
\[\Phi'\otimes\Psi'\longrightarrow(\mathbb Q/\mathbb Z)_{k'}\]

which is deduced from it by the base change \(k\longrightarrow k'\), and where \(E'_{\eta'}\) is the biextension of \((P'_{\eta'},Q'_{\eta'})\) by \(G_{m\eta'}\) deduced from the biextension \(E_\eta\) of \((P_\eta,Q_\eta)\) by \(G_{m\eta}\) by means of the base change \(\eta'\longrightarrow\eta\).
7.3.5.3. An interesting case is that where we have \(e(S'/S)=1\), i.e. where
\[\mathfrak m'=\mathfrak mV',\]

where \(\mathfrak m'\) denotes the maximal ideal of \(V'\). This is the case in particular if \(V'\) is étale over \(V\), or if it is the henselization, or the strict henselization, or the completion of \(V\). In this case the relation (7.3.5.2) is expressed simply by saying that the formation of the obstruction (7.1.1) resp. (7.1.2) commutes with the base change in question \(S'\longrightarrow S\).

7.3.5.4. On the other hand, the relation (7.3.5.2) in the general case shows that for every extension \(E_\eta\) as in 7.1 a) (resp. every biextension \(E_\eta\) as in 7.1 b)), there exists a finite morphism of traits \(S'\longrightarrow S\), such that \(k(\eta')\) is a separable extension of \(k(\eta)\), and such that the extension (resp. the biextension) \(E'_{\eta'}\) deduced from \(E_\eta\) by base change \(\eta'\longrightarrow\eta\) prolongs to an extension (resp. biextension) \(E'\) over all of \(S'\). Indeed, it suffices to choose \(S'/S\) such that \(e(S'/S)\) is a multiple of the integer \(n>0\) appearing in 7.1, and we know that we can always find such an extension, with \(k(\eta')\) separable over \(k(\eta)\).

Proposition 7.4. Let \(S\) be a reduced scheme.

a) Let \(P\) be a smooth group scheme over \(S\) whose fibers at the maximal points of \(S\) are connected. Then the natural functor

VII 1.3.4.1

\[(7.4.1)\qquad \operatorname{EXT}(P,G_{mS})\longrightarrow\operatorname{TORSRIG}(P,e_P;G_{mS})\]

from the category of extensions of \(P\) by \(G_{mS}\) to the category of torsors under \(G_{mP}\) rigidified with respect to the unit section of \(P\), is fully faithful. If \(S\) is normal, and \(P\) has connected fibers, then an object \(E\) of the second member of (7.4.1) belongs to the essential image of this functor if and only if for every maximal point \(\eta\) of \(S\), the rigidified torsor \(E_\eta\) over \(P_\eta\) comes from an extension of \(P_\eta\) by \(G_{m\eta}\).

b) Let \(P,Q\) be two smooth group schemes over \(S\) whose fibers at the maximal points of \(S\) are connected. Then the natural functor

VII 2.9.1

\[(7.4.2)\qquad \operatorname{BIEXT}(P,Q;G_{mS})\longrightarrow\operatorname{TORSBIRIG}(P,Q;G_{mS})\]

from the category of biextensions of \((P,Q)\) by \(G_{mS}\) to the category of torsors under \(G_{m,P\times_SQ}\) birigidified relatively to the pair of unit sections of \(P\) and of \(Q\), is fully faithful. If \(S\) is normal, and \(P\) or \(Q\) has connected fibers, an object \(E\) of the second member of (7.4.2) belongs to the essential image of the functor if and only if for every maximal point \(\eta\) of \(S\), \(E_\eta\) comes from a biextension of \((P_\eta,Q_\eta)\) by \(G_{m\eta}\).

We will prove a), the proof of b), essentially identical, being left to the reader. For the full faithfulness assertion, one may note that 4.1 remains valid if \(X\) and \(Y\) are schemes flat over a reduced scheme \(S\), whose fibers at the maximal points satisfy the conditions stated in 4.1: indeed, the family of maximal fibers of \(X\times_S Y\) (which is flat over the reduced \(S\)) is schematically dense in \(X\times_S Y\), so that a morphism from \(X\times_S Y\) to the separated \(S\)-scheme \(G_{mS}\) is trivial as soon as it is so on the maximal fibers. This said, the full faithfulness of (7.4.1) is proved like 4.2, and the characterization given there of the essential image remains equally valid. It remains to prove that if the rigidified torsor \(E\) over \(P\) is such that the \(E_\eta\) come from extensions, the same is true of \(E\), when we suppose \(S\) normal and \(P\) with connected fibers. This last condition implies that \(P\) is of finite presentation over \(S\) (SGA 3 VIB 5.5); the full faithfulness shows us on the other hand that the question is local on \(S\), which we may therefore suppose local affine, and the usual passage to the limit (using the theorem of NAGATA on the finiteness of the integral closure of a reduced \(\mathbb Z\)-algebra of finite type EGA IV 7.8) reduces us to the case where \(S\) is moreover assumed noetherian. Then 7.1 a) shows us that there exists an open set \(U\subset S\), whose complement is of codimension \(\geq2\), such that \(E_U\) comes from an extension of \(P_U\) by \(G_{mU}\). In other words, the sought-for isomorphism
\[m^*(E)\overset{\sim}\longrightarrow\operatorname{pr}_1^*(E)\operatorname{pr}_2^*(E)\]

can be constructed above \((P\times_S P)_U\). Now this is an open subset of the normal scheme \(P\times_S P\), whose complement is of codimension \(\geq2\). Hence the isomorphism in question over \((P\times_S P)_U\) prolongs to all of \(P\times_S P\), which completes the proof.

Corollary 7.5. Let \(S\) be a normal scheme, \(P\) and \(Q\) two group schemes smooth over \(S\), with connected maximal fibers, \(P\) or \(Q\) having connected fibers. Suppose moreover that for every maximal point \(\eta\) of \(S\), \(P_\eta\) or \(Q_\eta\) is an extension of an abelian scheme by a torus (more generally that it admits a composition series whose factors are abelian schemes, tori or groups \(G_a\)). Then the functor (7.4.2) is an equivalence of categories.
This is indeed an immediate consequence of 7.4 b) and of 4.7.
